The hospital retrieves the record and its Merkle inclusion proof. Every action is logged immutably on-chain, giving the patient a full audit trail. ### 4.4 Consensus Selection Logic for Health Blockchains ```mermaid flowchart LR A[New Health Transaction] --> B{Network Type} B -->|Public Global| C[Proof of Stake] B -->|Consortium Hospitals| D[PBFT or Raft] B -->|Single Hospital| E[Proof of Authority] B -->|Pharma Supply| F[Delegated PoS] C --> G[Validate and Commit] D --> G E --> G F --> G G --> H[Block Added to Chain] ``` > **Reading the diagram:** The consensus protocol is selected based on the network's trust model. Private hospital networks use PoA; multi-hospital consortiums use PBFT for deterministic finality; pharma supply chains use DPoS for high throughput. --- ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Style]** > *Define blockchain technology. List any THREE key features that make it suitable for health-informatics applications.* **Model Answer:** **Definition:** Blockchain is a distributed, decentralized, and immutable digital ledger that records transactions across a peer-to-peer network using cryptographic hashing and consensus mechanisms, without requiring a central trusted authority. **Three Key Features for Health Informatics:** 1. **Immutability:** Once a clinical record is written, it cannot be altered — providing tamper-evident audit trails for malpractice investigations and regulatory compliance. 2. **Decentralization:** Eliminates single points of failure and allows hospitals, labs, pharmacies, and patients to share data without a central server, improving interoperability. 3. **Patient-Controlled Consent:** Smart contracts let patients programmatically grant, revoke, and audit access to their EHR, addressing the consent-management gap in current health systems. > *(Any 3 of: immutability, transparency, decentralization, cryptographic security, smart contracts, auditability — credit awarded)* --- > **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Style]** > *What is a Merkle tree? Explain its role in verifying health-record integrity on a blockchain.* **Model Answer:** A **Merkle tree** is a binary tree of cryptographic hashes in which: - **Leaves** are hashes of individual transactions (e.g., patient records). - **Internal nodes** are hashes of the concatenation of their two child hashes. - The **Merkle root** sits at the top and is stored in the block header. **Role in Health-Record Integrity:** 1. The Merkle root in the block header cryptographically commits to *all* underlying transactions. 2. Any modification of even one bit in a single health record changes the leaf hash, cascading up to change the Merkle root. 3. Since the Merkle root is signed into the block and chained via the previous-hash link, historical clinical data becomes **tamper-evident**. 4. A hospital can prove a specific report is included in the chain using a **Merkle proof of length $\log_2 n$**, without downloading the entire ledger — enabling efficient SPV (Simplified Payment Verification) style health-record audits. --- ### 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each) > **Question A [KTU ESE Pattern — 14 Marks]** > > **(a)** *With a neat block-structure diagram, explain the anatomy of a block in a blockchain. Highlight the role of the previous block hash and Merkle root. (7 marks)* > > **(b)** *Discuss in detail FIVE major applications of blockchain technology in health informatics. For each, specify the problem addressed and the blockchain-based solution. (7 marks)* **Model Solution (a) — Block Anatomy (7 marks):** A block in a blockchain consists of two main parts: the **block header** and the **block body**. | Component | Content | Role | |---|---|---| | **Block Version** | Protocol version number | Forward compatibility | | **Previous Block Hash** | 256-bit hash of parent block header | **Chains blocks immutably** | | **Merkle Root** | Root hash of all transactions in the block | Commits to all tx data | | **Timestamp** | Unix time of block creation | Ordering and auditing | | **Difficulty Target** | $n$ leading zero bits required | PoW mining difficulty | | **Nonce** | 32-bit counter incremented during mining | Satisfies the difficulty target | | **Block Body** | List of validated transactions (Tx counter + tx list) | Stores actual clinical records/payloads | **Role of Previous Block Hash:** Creates the **cryptographic link** that forms the "chain." Any alteration to a historical block changes its hash, breaking the link to all subsequent blocks — a feature called **tamper-evidence**. **Role of Merkle Root:** Efficiently summarizes *all* transactions in the block in a single 256-bit value. Enables **lightweight verification** of individual record inclusion without processing the full block — critical for resource-constrained IoMT (Internet of Medical Things) devices. ``` +-------------------------------------------+ | BLOCK HEADER | +-------------------------------------------+ | Version : 1 | | Prev Block Hash: 0000ab12... | | Merkle Root : 9f8e7d6c... | | Timestamp : 1718234567 | | Difficulty : 0x1a2b3c4d | | Nonce : 2083236893 | +-------------------------------------------+ | BLOCK BODY | +-------------------------------------------+ | Tx Count : 4 | | Tx[0] : Patient consent grant | | Tx[1] : Lab report upload | | Tx[2] : Prescription record | | Tx[3] : Insurance claim trigger | +-------------------------------------------+ ``` **Model Solution (b) — Five Health-Informatics Applications (7 marks):** 1. **Electronic Health Records (EHR) Interoperability:** - **Problem:** Patient records are locked inside hospital silos; cross-hospital referrals are slow and error-prone. - **Solution:** A permissioned blockchain stores patient DIDs and encrypted pointers to off-chain records. Smart contracts automate access. *Example:* MedRec (MIT), Ankr Health. 2. **Pharmaceutical Supply Chain & Anti-Counterfeiting:** - **Problem:** WHO estimates 1 in 10 medical products in developing countries is substandard/falsified. - **Solution:** Each drug pack is tagged with a unique serial-number transaction on the chain. Manufacturers, distributors, and pharmacies append chain-of-custody entries. Consumers scan a QR code to verify provenance. *Example:* PharmaLedger, IBM Trust Chain. 3. **Clinical Trial Integrity:** - **Problem:** Selective reporting, protocol deviations, and data fabrication undermine trial validity. - **Solution:** Trial protocols, informed-consent signatures, and case-report forms are timestamped and hashed on-chain, creating an immutable audit trail. *Example:* Pfizer + IBM blockchain pilot. 4. **Insurance Claims Adjudication:** - **Problem:** Fraudulent claims cost billions annually; processing is slow. - **Solution:** Smart contracts auto-execute claim validation against policy rules and on-chain treatment records, releasing payment upon verification. *Example:* Aetna, Anthem pilots. 5. **Public Health Surveillance & Pandemic Response:** - **Problem:** Siloed national data delays outbreak detection and response. - **Solution:** Permissioned health-data consortiums (WHO, ECDC, ICMR nodes) share anonymized, aggregated disease metrics on-chain with full audit transparency. *Example:* WHO's blockchain-based MiPasa platform (COVID-19). > **Board Tip:** Each application must mention (i) the **problem** and (ii) the **blockchain-based solution** with a real or illustrative example to score full marks. --- > **Question B [KTU ESE Pattern — 14 Marks]** > > **(a)** *Explain the Proof-of-Work (PoW) and Proof-of-Stake (PoS) consensus mechanisms. Compare them on the basis of energy consumption, security, and suitability for health-data networks. (7 marks)* > > **(b)** *With a suitable diagram, explain how a Merkle tree is constructed from four clinical transactions. Show the step-by-step hash computations and derive the verification path length for n transactions. (7 marks)* **Model Solution (a) — PoW vs. PoS (7 marks):** **Proof of Work (PoW):** - Validators (miners) compete to solve a computational puzzle: find a nonce such that `SHA-256(Block Header)` has a target number of leading zero bits. - The first to find a valid nonce broadcasts the block; the network accepts it after verification. - Security relies on the **computational cost of attack** — an attacker needs > 50% of the network's total hash rate to rewrite history. - Used by Bitcoin; extremely energy-intensive (~150 TWh/year). **Proof of Stake (PoS):** - Validators are *selected* to propose blocks proportional to the number of tokens they have *staked* (locked as collateral). - Misbehavior results in **slashing** — forfeiture of staked tokens — providing cryptoeconomic security. - Energy consumption is ~99.95% lower than PoW. - Used by Ethereum (post-Merge), Cardano, Polkadot. **Comparison Table:** | Criterion | PoW | PoS | |---|---|---| | Energy Consumption | **Very High** | **Very Low** | | Security Model | Computational cost | Economic stake (slashing) | | TPS | ~7 (Bitcoin) | ~1000+ (Ethereum 2.0) | | Finality | Probabilistic (needs confirmations) | Often deterministic / economic | | Suitability for Health Networks | **Poor** (latency, energy) | **Good** (public health chains) | | Suitability for EHR | **Poor** | **Acceptable** | | Best Health Use | Rare | Public surveillance, pharma tracking | **Suitability Verdict for Health-Data Networks:** - **PoW** is **not recommended** for clinical/health data networks due to latency, energy cost, and low throughput. - **PoS** is suitable for public, large-scale health networks (pandemic tracking, pharma supply chain). - For **EHR consortiums**, **PBFT or Raft** (not PoW/PoS) are preferred for deterministic finality and regulator-friendly auditability. --- **Model Solution (b) — Merkle Tree Construction (7 marks):** **Setup:** Four clinical transactions $T_1, T_2, T_3, T_4$ representing: lab report, prescription, consent, and imaging metadata. **Step 1 — Hash each transaction (leaf level):** $$ H_1 = \text{SHA-256}(T_1), \quad H_2 = \text{SHA-256}(T_2), \quad H_3 = \text{SHA-256}(T_3), \quad H_4 = \text{SHA-256}(T_4) $$ **Step 2 — Hash pairs of leaves (intermediate level):** $$ H_{12} = \text{SHA-256}(H_1 \,\vert\vert\, H_2), \quad H_{34} = \text{SHA-256}(H_3 \,\vert\vert\, H_4) $$ **Step 3 — Hash the intermediate nodes to obtain the Merkle Root:** $$ H_{\text{root}} = \text{SHA-256}(H_{12} \,\vert\vert\, H_{34}) $$ **Diagram:** ``` H_root / \ H_12 H_34 / \ / \ H_1 H_2 H_3 H_4 | | | | T_1 T_2 T_3 T_4 ``` **Derivation of verification path length for $n$ transactions:** A Merkle tree is a complete binary tree. With $n$ leaves: - Number of levels (depth) = $\lceil \log_2 n \rceil$ - To verify inclusion of a specific leaf, a verifier needs the sibling hash at *each level* — exactly **one hash per level**. - Therefore, the **Merkle proof length** is: $$ V(n) = \log_2 n $$ **Substituting $n = 4$:** $$ V(4) = \log_2 4 = 2 $$ So to prove $T_2$ is in the block, a verifier needs only $H_1$, $H_{34}$, and the Merkle root — **2 hashes**, instead of downloading all 4 transactions. **Scalability Insight:** Even for $n = 1{,}000{,}000$ transactions, $V(n) = \log_2(10^6) \approx 20$ hashes — making blockchain audit verification practical for massive health-data consortiums. --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in Blockchain Questions:** > > 1. **Confusing "decentralized" with "anonymous"** — Blockchain is pseudonymous, not anonymous. Real-world health systems **must** implement KYC/identity verification for HIPAA compliance. Writing "blockchain is anonymous" in an exam costs 1–2 marks. > > 2. **Forgetting the off-chain storage point** — Writing that "all patient data is stored on the blockchain" is technically and economically wrong. Examiners expect students to say: *encrypted data on off-chain storage, hash pointer on-chain.* Failing to mention this is a frequent 2-mark loss. > > 3. **Merkle root missing a step** — When asked to "construct a Merkle tree," students often compute only leaf hashes and skip the intermediate concatenation step. Always show **all three levels**: leaves, intermediate nodes, and root. > > 4. **Mishandling consensus comparison** — Saying "PoS is faster than PoW" is incomplete. Examiners want **numbers** (e.g., "PoS achieves ~1000 TPS vs. PoW's ~7 TPS") and **a health-specific justification**. > > 5. **Ignoring regulatory context** — A health-informatics answer that omits HIPAA/GDPR/DISHA compliance references will be marked down. Always tie technical features to **regulatory benefit**. > > 6. **Equation formatting errors** — Writing $\vert H \vert$ (with bare pipes) inside a markdown table breaks the table. Use `\vert` or `\mid` for vertical bars in LaTeX inside tables. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Module 1 — Blockchain in Health Informatics:** - **Core definition:** Blockchain = distributed, decentralized, immutable, cryptographically chained ledger. - **Block structure:** Header (version, prev hash, Merkle root, timestamp, difficulty, nonce) + Body (transactions). - **Hashing function:** SHA-256 (256-bit output); one-way and collision-resistant. - **Merkle root** cryptographically commits to all transactions in a block; depth = $\lceil \log_2 n \rceil$; verification path = $\log_2 n$. - **Consensus protocols:** PoW (high security, very high energy, low TPS) ❌ for health; PoS (lower energy, moderate TPS) ⚠️; **PBFT/Raft (deterministic, low latency, high TPS) ✅ for EHR consortiums**; PoA ✅ for private hospital chains; DPoS ✅ for pharma supply chains. - **Double-spend attack probability:** $P = (q/p)^z$; after 6 confirmations, even a 10% attacker has < 0.0002% success. - **On-chain vs. off-chain:** Bulky data (EHR, imaging) goes to encrypted off-chain storage; only hashes/pointers/consent live on-chain. - **Health-informatics applications (must-know five):** EHR interoperability, pharma supply chain, clinical trial integrity, insurance claims, public health surveillance. - **Standards & compliance:** HIPAA (US), GDPR (EU), DISHA (India), HL7 FHIR (interoperability), W3C DID (identity), W3C VC (credentials). - **Smart contracts:** Self-executing code for consent, billing, and access control; deployed on Ethereum, Hyperledger Fabric. - **Block time:** Bitcoin ~10 min, Ethereum ~12–15 s, Hyperledger ~sub-second — choose based on clinical latency requirements. - **Key advantage over traditional DBs:** Tamper-evidence, decentralized trust, native interoperability, automated patient consent. - **Key limitation:** Throughput, energy (for PoW), storage growth, regulatory ambiguity, and the *oracle problem* (how real-world clinical events enter the chain). ---

topics.title: Clinical Research Informatics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEDAT751 - COMPUTATIONAL HEALTH INFORMATICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Clinical Research Informatics

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Clinical Research Informatics (CRI)** is the sub-discipline of biomedical informatics that deals with the design, development, deployment, and management of information technology (IT) systems, data standards, and computational methods applied to **clinical research workflows**, including clinical trials, observational studies, translational research, and secondary use of clinical data for scientific discovery.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, CRI sits at the intersection of three knowledge pillars:

| Pillar | Role in CRI |
|---|---|
| **Clinical Medicine** | Provides disease phenotypes, protocols, and patient cohorts |
| **Computer Science** | Provides databases, algorithms, NLP, and AI pipelines |
| **Regulatory Science** | Governs compliance (FDA 21 CFR Part 11, ICH-GCP, IRB) |

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Airport Control Tower

Imagine a **busy international airport**:
- **Air Traffic Controllers** = CRI Data Managers — they guide the safe takeoff (study launch), flight (study conduct), and landing (study closeout) of every clinical research "aircraft."
- **Radar Screens** = Electronic Data Capture (EDC) systems — they show in real time where every data point, patient, and protocol deviation is happening.
- **Flight Plans** = Clinical Trial Protocols (ICH-GCP compliant).
- **Runways** = Hospital EHR systems that supply patient data for research.
- **Weather Reports** = Biostatistical Monitoring Reports and Data Safety Monitoring Boards (DSMB).

Just as no modern airport operates without a control tower, **no modern clinical trial can be executed at scale without CRI infrastructure**.

### 1.3 Scope Boundaries

> [!NOTE]
> **Clinical Informatics ≠ Clinical Research Informatics.**
> - **Clinical Informatics** → optimizes care delivery for a *single patient in front of you today*.
> - **Clinical Research Informatics** → optimizes knowledge generation for *populations of future patients*.

### 1.4 Standard Metrics Used in CRI

- **Sensitivity (Sn)** — ability to correctly identify a true case from EHR data.
- **Specificity (Sp)** — ability to correctly reject a non-case.
- **Positive Predictive Value (PPV)** — probability that a flagged case is truly a case.
- **Number Needed to Screen (NNS)** — patients screened to recruit 1 trial subject.
- **eCRF** — electronic Case Report Form (standard data capture instrument).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The CRI Knowledge Stack — Layered Architecture

CRI is built as a 5-layer stack. Each layer depends on the layer below it.

1. **Layer 1 — Regulatory Foundation**
   Anchors every process in **ICH-GCP E6(R3)**, **21 CFR Part 11**, and **HIPAA Privacy Rule (45 CFR 164)**. Without this, no data is admissible to FDA/EMA review.

2. **Layer 2 — Data Standards**
   - **CDISC** family: CDASH (data collection), SDTM (submission tabulation), ADaM (analysis).
   - **HL7 FHIR** for inter-operable EHR-to-research data exchange.
   - **OMOP CDM** (Observational Medical Outcomes Partnership Common Data Model) for secondary use.

3. **Layer 3 — Data Acquisition Systems**
   - **EDC** (Electronic Data Capture, e.g., REDCap, Medidata Rave, Oracle InForm).
   - **ePRO** (electronic Patient-Reported Outcomes).
   - **EHR-to-EDC** integration via FHIR/HL7.

4. **Layer 4 — Analytics & Translational Pipelines**
   - Phenotyping algorithms.
   - NLP extraction from clinical notes.
   - Genomic + clinical data linkage.

5. **Layer 5 — Reporting & Regulatory Submission**
   - Clinical Study Reports (CSR).
   - Statistical Analysis Plan (SAP) outputs.
   - Public registry uploads (ClinicalTrials.gov, CTRI).

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas are written using `\vert` or `\mid` for absolute-value/conditional bars to preserve markdown table syntax integrity.

| # | Formula / Concept | Symbolic Form | Domain / Application | Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Sample size for two-proportion comparison | $n = \dfrac{(Z_{\alpha/2} + Z_{\beta})^{2} \cdot [p_{1}(1-p_{1}) + p_{2}(1-p_{2})]}{(p_{1}-p_{2})^{2}}$ | Phase III trial design | patients per arm |
| 2 | Statistical Power | $\text{Power} = 1 - \beta$ | Pre-trial planning | dimensionless |
| 3 | Sensitivity | $Sn = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FN}$ | EHR phenotyping evaluation | dimensionless (0–1) |
| 4 | Specificity | $Sp = \dfrac{TN}{TN + FP}$ | EHR phenotyping evaluation | dimensionless (0–1) |
| 5 | Positive Predictive Value | $PPV = \dfrac{Sn \cdot P}{Sn \cdot P + (1-Sp)(1-P)}$ | Bayes-adapted for prevalence $P$ | dimensionless (0–1) |
| 6 | Number Needed to Screen | $NNS = \dfrac{1}{PPV}$ | Trial recruitment efficiency | patients / recruit |
| 7 | Hazard Ratio (Cox) | $HR(t) = \dfrac{h_{1}(t)}{h_{2}(t)}$ | Survival analysis reporting | dimensionless |
| 8 | Confidence Interval (95%) | $CI = \bar{x} \pm 1.96 \cdot \dfrac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}$ | Continuous outcome reporting | study-specific |
| 9 | Inter-rater Agreement | $\kappa = \dfrac{p_{o}-p_{e}}{1-p_{e}}$ | Annotator consistency in NLP | dimensionless (–1 to 1) |
| 10 | Data Query Rate | $DQR = \dfrac{\text{Open Queries}}{\text{Total Fields}}$ | Site data quality monitoring | dimensionless (0–1) |

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

CRI is the silent engine behind every drug approved by the **U.S. FDA** and **EMA**. For example, the mRNA COVID-19 vaccines (Pfizer-BioNTech, Moderna) relied on CRI systems managing **>40,000 subjects**, **>2,000 sites**, and **>10 million data points** in real time under pandemic compression. Without CRI, modern evidence-based medicine would not exist at population scale.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following derivations use the **aligned math block** format with explanatory text rows. Every algebraic transition is explicitly justified for KTU ESE valuation.

### 3.1 Derivation of Sample Size for Two Independent Proportions

We want to test:
$$H_{0}: p_{1} = p_{2} \quad \text{vs.} \quad H_{1}: p_{1} \neq p_{2}$$

**Step 1 — Define the test statistic.**
The standardized difference between two independent sample proportions is:
$$Z = \dfrac{(\hat{p}_{1} - \hat{p}_{2}) - 0}{\sqrt{\dfrac{\hat{p}_{1}(1-\hat{p}_{1})}{n} + \dfrac{\hat{p}_{2}(1-\hat{p}_{2})}{n}}}$$

*Explanation:* Under $H_{0}$, the numerator mean is 0; the denominator is the pooled standard error assuming equal sample sizes $n$ per arm.

**Step 2 — Reject $H_{0}$ when the observed effect exceeds the critical value.**
We reject when $\vert Z \vert \ge Z_{\alpha/2}$.

**Step 3 — Express the smallest detectable effect $\Delta = \vert p_{1} - p_{2} \vert$ we wish to detect with probability $1-\beta$.**
Setting the non-central standardized distance equal to $Z_{\alpha/2} + Z_{\beta}$:
$$\Delta = (Z_{\alpha/2} + Z_{\beta})\sqrt{\dfrac{p_{1}(1-p_{1}) + p_{2}(1-p_{2})}{n}}$$

*Explanation:* The quantity $Z_{\alpha/2}$ controls Type-I error (false positive), $Z_{\beta}$ controls Type-II error (false negative). We add them because we need the effect to clear both thresholds simultaneously.

**Step 4 — Solve algebraically for $n$.**
Squaring both sides:
$$\Delta^{2} = (Z_{\alpha/2} + Z_{\beta})^{2} \cdot \dfrac{p_{1}(1-p_{1}) + p_{2}(1-p_{2})}{n}$$

Rearranging for $n$:
$$n = \dfrac{(Z_{\alpha/2} + Z_{\beta})^{2} \cdot [p_{1}(1-p_{1}) + p_{2}(1-p_{2})]}{(p_{1}-p_{2})^{2}}$$

*Explanation:* This is the **per-arm sample size**. The total trial size is $2n$ for a two-arm parallel design.

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example

**Problem:** A Phase III trial compares a new antihypertensive (Arm A) versus standard care (Arm B). Expected response rates are $p_{1} = 0.70$ and $p_{2} = 0.60$. Take $\alpha = 0.05$ (two-sided) and Power $= 80\%$. Compute $n$ per arm.

**Step 1 — Identify critical values.**
For $\alpha/2 = 0.025$, $Z_{\alpha/2} = 1.96$.
For Power $= 0.80$, $\beta = 0.20$, so $Z_{\beta} = 0.84$.

**Step 2 — Substitute values.**
$$\begin{aligned}
n &= \dfrac{(1.96 + 0.84)^{2} \cdot [0.70 \cdot 0.30 + 0.60 \cdot 0.40]}{(0.70 - 0.60)^{2}} \\
&= \dfrac{(2.80)^{2} \cdot [0.21 + 0.24]}{(0.10)^{2}} \\
&= \dfrac{7.84 \cdot 0.45}{0.01} \\
&= \dfrac{3.528}{0.01} \\
&= 352.8
\end{aligned}$$

*Explanation:* Always round up to the next whole patient to preserve the desired power. Thus **$n = 353$ per arm**, total enrollment $\approx 706$ subjects.

### 3.3 Derivation of PPV from Bayes' Theorem

**Step 1 — Start with the definition of PPV.**
$$PPV = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FP}$$

**Step 2 — Express $TP$ and $FP$ in terms of Sensitivity, Specificity, and Prevalence $P$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
TP &= Sn \cdot P \cdot N \\
FP &= (1 - Sp) \cdot (1 - P) \cdot N
\end{aligned}
$$

*Explanation:* $N$ is the total screened population. $Sn \cdot P$ fraction is the true positive rate per person; multiplied by $N$ gives absolute $TP$ count.

**Step 3 — Substitute and cancel $N$.**
$$PPV = \dfrac{Sn \cdot P \cdot N}{Sn \cdot P \cdot N + (1 - Sp)(1 - P) \cdot N} = \dfrac{Sn \cdot P}{Sn \cdot P + (1 - Sp)(1 - P)}$$

*Explanation:* This is the **Bayes-adapted PPV** — it explicitly shows how a phenotype's predictive value changes with disease prevalence in the screened population, which is critical when moving a phenotyping algorithm from a tertiary hospital (high prevalence) to a primary-care setting (low prevalence).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]
> All Mermaid node IDs are alphanumeric, prefixed with letters (e.g., `nodeA1`, `stepB2`), and labels contain only clean uppercase alphanumeric text. No markdown formatting, no HTML tables, and no reserved keywords are used inside double-quoted labels.

### 4.1 CRI End-to-End Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeA1[REGULATORY LAYER ICH GCP 21 CFR PART 11 HIPAA]
    nodeA2[DATA STANDARDS LAYER CDISC SDTM ADAM HL7 FHIR OMOP CDM]
    nodeA3[ACQUISITION LAYER EDC EPRO EHR FHIR ADAPTERS]
    nodeA4[ANALYTICS LAYER PHENOTYPING NLP MACHINE LEARNING BIOBANKS]
    nodeA5[REPORTING LAYER CSR SAP CLINICALTRIALS GOV DSMB]
    nodeB1[PATIENT AT POINT OF CARE]
    nodeB2[INVESTIGATOR AT SITE]
    nodeB3[DATA MANAGER AT SPONSOR]
    nodeB4[STATISTICIAN AT CRO]
    nodeB5[REGULATOR AT FDA OR EMA]

    nodeA1 --> nodeA2
    nodeA2 --> nodeA3
    nodeA3 --> nodeA4
    nodeA4 --> nodeA5

    nodeB1 --> nodeA3
    nodeB2 --> nodeA3
    nodeB3 --> nodeA2
    nodeB4 --> nodeA4
    nodeB5 --> nodeA5
```

### 4.2 Clinical Trial Data Lifecycle in CRI

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    stepA1[STUDY DESIGN PROTOCOL SAP]
    stepA2[IRB APPROVAL]
    stepA3[SITE INITIATION]
    stepA4[PATIENT RECRUITMENT EHR TRIAL MATCHING]
    stepA5[DATA CAPTURE ECRF EPRO]
    stepA6[DATA CLEANING QUERY MANAGEMENT]
    stepA7[INTERIM ANALYSIS DSMB REVIEW]
    stepA8[DATABASE LOCK]
    stepA9[FINAL CSR SDTM PACKAGE]
    stepA10[REGULATORY SUBMISSION FDA EMA]

    stepA1 --> stepA2 --> stepA3 --> stepA4 --> stepA5 --> stepA6 --> stepA7 --> stepA8 --> stepA9 --> stepA10
    stepA10 -.feedback.-> stepA1
```

### 4.3 Secondary-Use of EHR Data for Research Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    srcA[EHR CLINICAL NOTES LAB IMAGING]
    srcB[WEARABLES AND PATIENT APPS]
    srcC[BIOBANK GENOMIC DATA]
    procA[DE IDENTIFICATION HIPAA SAFE HARBOR]
    procB[NLP AND PHENOTYPING]
    procC[DATA HARMONIZATION TO OMOP CDM]
    outA[COHORT DISCOVERY]
    outB[OBSERVATIONAL STUDY]
    outC[REAL WORLD EVIDENCE RWE]
    outD[MACHINE LEARNING MODEL]

    srcA --> procA
    srcB --> procA
    srcC --> procA
    procA --> procB --> procC
    procC --> outA
    procC --> outB
    procC --> outC
    procC --> outD
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**Define Clinical Research Informatics (CRI). List any four functional areas it covers.**

**Model Answer:**
Clinical Research Informatics (CRI) is the sub-discipline of biomedical informatics focused on applying information science, computer science, and statistical methods to **clinical research workflows** — particularly clinical trials, observational studies, and translational research.

Four functional areas:
1. **Clinical trial management systems (CTMS).**
2. **Electronic Data Capture (EDC) for case report forms.**
3. **Biobank informatics and biospecimen tracking.**
4. **Regulatory submission informatics (e.g., CDISC SDTM packaging).**

---

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**What is the difference between Clinical Informatics and Clinical Research Informatics? Give one example use-case of each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Clinical Informatics | Clinical Research Informatics |
|---|---|---|
| **Goal** | Optimize care for individual patients | Generate new knowledge for populations |
| **User** | Treating clinician | Researcher / sponsor / regulator |
| **Example** | Real-time drug-allergy alerts in EHR | REDCap database for a Phase II oncology trial |

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU ESE Module pattern: **ONE question is mandatory**; you must answer the *other* by choosing between **Question A** or **Question B**. Both are provided below.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Module 1 Choice 1]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **CDISC data standards stack** (CDASH, SDTM, ADaM). How do they support regulatory submission to FDA?

**(b) [7 Marks]** A clinical phenotyping algorithm has Sensitivity = 0.92 and Specificity = 0.88. In a tertiary hospital, disease prevalence is 8%. (i) Calculate PPV. (ii) If the same algorithm is deployed in a primary-care clinic where prevalence is 2%, find the new PPV. (iii) Comment on the implications for trial recruitment.

##### **Model Answer to (a):**

The CDISC stack is a **3-tier pipeline**:

1. **CDASH (Clinical Data Acquisition Standards Harmonization)** — Defines the *variable names and metadata* used at the point of data collection (CRF design). Ensures every site captures data uniformly.

2. **SDTM (Study Data Tabulation Model)** — Defines the *submission-ready dataset structure* with standardized domains (DM = Demographics, AE = Adverse Events, LB = Lab). One row = one observation per subject per timepoint.

3. **ADaM (Analysis Data Model)** — Defines the *analysis-ready datasets* with traceability flags (e.g., ADSL = Subject-Level Analysis Dataset, ADEVENT = Adverse Event Analysis Dataset).

**Submission support:** The FDA mandates CDISC-compliant SDTM/ADaM datasets via the **eCTD (electronic Common Technical Document)**. The traceability from raw CRF (CDASH) → SDTM → ADaM → CSR allows FDA reviewers to reconstruct the entire evidence chain — fulfilling 21 CFR Part 11 auditability.

##### **Model Answer to (b):**

**(i) Tertiary hospital PPV (P = 0.08):**
$$\begin{aligned}
PPV_{1} &= \dfrac{0.92 \cdot 0.08}{0.92 \cdot 0.08 + 0.12 \cdot 0.92} \\
&= \dfrac{0.0736}{0.0736 + 0.1104} \\
&= \dfrac{0.0736}{0.1840} \\
&= 0.40
\end{aligned}$$

*Explanation:* Even with a strong algorithm, only 40% of flagged positives are true cases because prevalence is low.

**(ii) Primary-care clinic PPV (P = 0.02):**
$$\begin{aligned}
PPV_{2} &= \dfrac{0.92 \cdot 0.02}{0.92 \cdot 0.02 + 0.12 \cdot 0.98} \\
&= \dfrac{0.0184}{0.0184 + 0.1176} \\
&= \dfrac{0.0184}{0.1360} \\
&\approx 0.135
\end{aligned}$$

**(iii) Implications:**
- **PPV drops from 40% → 13.5%** as prevalence decreases.
- **NNS rises from ~2.5 to ~7.4** — meaning the recruitment team must screen 3× more patients.
- **Strategy:** Restrict algorithm deployment to high-prevalence cohorts, or use a *two-stage screening* design (cheap low-Sn/Sp algorithm first → manual chart review second).

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Module 1 Choice 2]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Discuss the role of **EHR-based phenotyping** in clinical research. What are the challenges of using routine clinical data for research?

**(b) [7 Marks]** A Phase III trial expects $p_{1}=0.65$ in the treatment arm and $p_{2}=0.55$ in the control arm. For $\alpha=0.05$ (two-sided) and Power = 90%, calculate the per-arm sample size.

##### **Model Answer to (a):**

**Role of EHR-Based Phenotyping:**
Phenotyping algorithms identify patients with a specific disease or trait from EHR data using structured (ICD codes, lab values) and unstructured (clinical notes, via NLP) data. They enable:
- **Cohort discovery** at scale across millions of records.
- **Trial feasibility analysis** (how many eligible patients exist).
- **Real-world evidence (RWE)** generation.
- **Automated pre-screening** for trial recruitment.

**Challenges:**
1. **Data heterogeneity** — different hospitals use different codes and documentation styles.
2. **Missing data** — clinical care prioritizes treatment, not completeness.
3. **Bias** — EHR data reflects only patients who sought care.
4. **Temporal validity** — a "diabetes" code 5 years ago may not reflect current status.
5. **Privacy** — HIPAA/GDPR require de-identification.
6. **Validation burden** — every algorithm needs chart-review gold-standard evaluation (Cohen's $\kappa$).

##### **Model Answer to (b):**

**Given:** $p_{1}=0.65$, $p_{2}=0.55$, $\alpha=0.05$ two-sided → $Z_{\alpha/2}=1.96$, Power $= 0.90$ → $Z_{\beta}=1.28$.

**Step 1 — Sum critical values.**
$$Z_{\alpha/2} + Z_{\beta} = 1.96 + 1.28 = 3.24$$

**Step 2 — Compute variance terms.**
$$\begin{aligned}
p_{1}(1-p_{1}) &= 0.65 \cdot 0.35 = 0.2275 \\
p_{2}(1-p_{2}) &= 0.55 \cdot 0.45 = 0.2475 \\
\text{Sum} &= 0.4750
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Square the effect size.**
$$(p_{1} - p_{2})^{2} = (0.10)^{2} = 0.01$$

**Step 4 — Combine and solve.**
$$\begin{aligned}
n &= \dfrac{(3.24)^{2} \cdot 0.4750}{0.01} \\
&= \dfrac{10.4976 \cdot 0.4750}{0.01} \\
&= \dfrac{4.98636}{0.01} \\
&= 498.636
\end{aligned}$$

**Final answer:** $n \approx 499$ per arm; **total enrollment $\approx 998$ subjects.**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> - **Do not** forget to *round up* $n$ to the next whole number (valuing 0.5 marks).
> - **Do not** confuse one-sided vs two-sided $\alpha$ when reading the critical value.
> - **Do not** omit the *clinical interpretation* in part-(b) questions — KTU now awards marks for translational reasoning under NEP-2020 outcome-based assessment.
> - **Always** state the regulatory framework (21 CFR Part 11, ICH-GCP, HIPAA) when discussing CRI systems.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- 🔑 **CRI** is the informatics discipline applied to *clinical research* — distinct from clinical-care informatics.
- 🔑 The **CDISC stack** (CDASH → SDTM → ADaM) is the backbone of regulatory submissions.
- 🔑 **HL7 FHIR** is the modern bridge between EHRs and research systems.
- 🔑 **Sample size** depends on $\alpha$, Power, and effect size; smaller effects require exponentially larger $n$.
- 🔑 **PPV is prevalence-dependent** — an algorithm with $Sn=0.92$, $Sp=0.88$ has very different utility in tertiary vs primary-care settings.
- 🔑 The **clinical trial lifecycle** is: Design → IRB → Initiation → Recruitment → Capture → Cleaning → Interim Analysis → Lock → CSR → Submission.
- 🔑 **21 CFR Part 11** mandates audit trails, electronic signatures, and data integrity for FDA submissions.
- 🔑 **Biobanks** require CRI support for specimen lifecycle tracking (collection → processing → storage → distribution).
- 🔑 **Secondary use of EHR data** powers Real-World Evidence (RWE) and observational research.
- 🔑 KTU valuation favors **formula derivation with interpretation**, not just numerical answers.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: IoT Devices for Healthcare
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEDAT751 - COMPUTATIONAL HEALTH INFORMATICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** IoT Devices for Healthcare

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Internet of Things (IoT) in Healthcare** is defined as an interconnected ecosystem of medical-grade sensors, actuators, wearable/implantable devices, communication gateways, and cloud-edge computing platforms that seamlessly collect, transmit, process, and visualize physiological and environmental patient data to enable continuous monitoring, predictive diagnostics, telemedicine, and personalized therapeutic interventions.

In KTU 2024 Scheme parlance, the term "Computational Health Informatics" treats IoT devices as the **physical data-acquisition edge** of a broader Cyber-Physical System (CPS) that bridges the human body, the digital record (EHR/EMR), and the clinical decision-support pipeline.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Hospital That Lives in Your Pocket"

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition Box:** Imagine a tiny invisible nurse attached to your wrist 24/7. This nurse silently measures your heartbeats, oxygen levels, sleep cycles, calories, and even stress hormones — and whispers all of it to a doctor sitting 1,000 km away in a clinic. The doctor doesn't need to see you physically; the data and AI do the talking. That invisible nurse is an **IoT Healthcare Device**.

Just as a smartphone replaced the combined roles of a camera, calculator, music player, and map, an **IoT healthcare device** consolidates the functions of a stethoscope, a thermometer, a heart-rate monitor, a glucometer, and an ECG machine into a single, network-connected micro-system.

### 1.3 Key Standard Metrics & Healthcare Benchmarks

| Metric | Standard Value | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **Sampling Frequency (Fs)** | **250 Hz – 1000 Hz** for ECG | Nyquist criterion for cardiac signals |
| **Data Rate** | **10 kbps – 1 Mbps** | Bluetooth Low Energy (BLE) baseline |
| **Battery Life** | **>72 hours** continuous | Clinical-grade wearable benchmark |
| **Latency** | **< 200 ms** | Real-time alarm threshold |
| **Data Accuracy** | **± 5 %** vs. clinical gold standard | FDA Class II medical device tolerance |
| **Wireless Range** | **5 m – 10 m** (BLE) | Body-Area-Network (BAN) radius |

### 1.4 Categorization of Healthcare IoT Devices

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight — Four Functional Categories**
>
> 1. **Wearables** — Smartwatches, fitness bands, smart patches (external body contact).
> 2. **Implantables** — Pacemakers, cochlear implants, smart pills (subcutaneous or ingestible).
> 3. **Ambient / Stationary** — Smart beds, fall-detection floors, in-room environmental sensors.
> 4. **Mobile / Handheld** — Smartphone-connected stethoscopes, portable ultrasound, pulse oximeters.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet

### 2.1 The Four-Layer Reference Architecture of Healthcare IoT

A healthcare-grade IoT system is universally described as a **4-layer stack** (often extended to 5 layers in KTU board questions). Each layer has a specific role, standard protocols, and failure modes.

#### Layer 1 — Perception / Sensor Layer (The "Nervous System")
- Comprises **biosensors, physical sensors, chemical sensors** that convert biological signals into electrical equivalents.
- Examples: PPG (photoplethysmography) for SpO₂, ECG electrodes, MEMS accelerometers, sweat-glucose enzymatic sensors.
- Key equation: Output voltage of a Wheatstone-bridge biosensor
$$V_{out} = V_{ex} \cdot \frac{\Delta R}{4R}$$

#### Layer 2 — Network / Communication Layer (The "Circulatory System")
- Handles **data transport** from sensor to gateway using short-range or long-range protocols.
- Common protocols and their trade-offs:

| Protocol | Frequency Band | Range | Power | Typical Healthcare Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **BLE (Bluetooth Low Energy)** | 2.4 GHz | 10 m | Very Low | Wearables, patches |
| **ZigBee (IEEE 802.15.4)** | 2.4 GHz | 100 m | Low | In-hospital mesh |
| **Wi-Fi (IEEE 802.11)** | 2.4 / 5 GHz | 50 m | Medium | Hospital infrastructure |
| **LoRaWAN** | 868 / 915 MHz | 2–10 km | Very Low | Remote rural patient monitoring |
| **NB-IoT (LTE Cat-NB1)** | Cellular | >10 km | Low | Asset & ambulance tracking |
| **NFC** | 13.56 MHz | <10 cm | Ultra-Low | Smart pill verification |

#### Layer 3 — Processing / Edge-Cloud Layer (The "Cerebral Cortex")
- Performs **data aggregation, denoising, feature extraction, encryption, and AI inference**.
- Three tiers:
  - **Edge** (microcontroller on-device)
  - **Fog** (local gateway / Raspberry-Pi class)
  - **Cloud** (AWS HealthLake, Azure Health Data Services)

#### Layer 4 — Application Layer (The "User Interface")
- Delivers value to the **end user** — patient, clinician, hospital administrator, or researcher.
- Apps: Remote Patient Monitoring (RPM), Chronic Care Management (CCM), Hospital-at-Home, Clinical Trials.

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Yield Equations for KTU 2024 End-Semester Exam**

| # | Equation | Meaning | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $Fs \geq 2 \cdot F_{max}$ | Nyquist sampling theorem for biomedical signals | Hz |
| 2 | $P_{total} = P_{sensor} + P_{MCU} + P_{radio}$ | Total node power budget | mW |
| 3 | $T_{battery} = \frac{C_{mAh}}{I_{avg}}$ | Battery life estimation | hours |
| 4 | $SNR_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{V_{signal}}{V_{noise}}\right)$ | Signal-to-noise ratio for biosensor fidelity | dB |
| 5 | $D_{throughput} = N_{samples} \times W_{bits} \times F_{s}$ | Required wireless data rate | bps |
| 6 | $E_{bit} = \dfrac{P_{radio}}{D_{throughput}}$ | Energy efficiency of radio | J/bit |
| 7 | $V_{out} = V_{ex}\dfrac{\Delta R}{4R}$ | Wheatstone-bridge biosensor output | V |

> **Engineering Utility (Real World):** These seven equations cover ~ 90 % of numerical problems KTU examiners pose on healthcare IoT — from calculating how long a smartwatch can record ECG on a 200 mAh battery, to whether a LoRa link can carry the bandwidth of a continuous glucose monitor.

### 2.3 Design Constraints — "The Healthcare IoT Trilemma"

Every IoT medical device must simultaneously balance three conflicting engineering demands, often referred to as the **trilemma**:

1. **Miniaturization (Size)** ⇄ **Battery Life (Energy)** ⇄ **Computational Power (Intelligence)**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Golden Trade-off Rule:** Smaller device → smaller battery → less energy available. To compensate, designers must use ultra-low-power MCUs (e.g., Nordic nRF5340, TI MSP430), duty-cycled radios, and on-chip AI inference rather than cloud round-trips.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Analytical Derivations & Worked Examples

> **Examiner's Intent:** KTU 2024 Scheme Module-1 problems typically fall into three categories — (a) Sampling & bandwidth, (b) Battery & power budgeting, (c) Architecture & protocol selection. The three worked derivations below cover each.

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Sampling Frequency & Data Throughput for a Wearable ECG

**Given Scenario:** A wearable chest-strap ECG patch samples the cardiac signal whose highest meaningful frequency component is 150 Hz (the QRS complex contains harmonics up to ~ 150 Hz in clinical-grade recordings). Each sample is quantized to **12 bits**, and the device transmits over BLE.

**Goal:** Compute the minimum sampling rate and the required wireless throughput.

**Step 1 — Apply Nyquist's Sampling Theorem.**
The Nyquist criterion states that the sampling frequency must be at least twice the maximum frequency present in the signal to avoid aliasing.

$$\begin{aligned}
F_s &\geq 2 \times F_{max} \\
F_s &\geq 2 \times 150\ \text{Hz} \\
F_s &\geq 300\ \text{Hz}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Add a Safety Margin (Engineering Best Practice).**
Clinical ECG standards (IEC 60601-2-47) recommend a 25 % margin above Nyquist to accommodate filter roll-off and inter-sample variability.

$$\begin{aligned}
F_{s,\text{clinical}} &= 1.25 \times 300 \\
F_{s,\text{clinical}} &= 375\ \text{Hz}
\end{aligned}$$

A standard of **500 Hz** is universally adopted for diagnostic ECG.

**Step 3 — Compute Wireless Throughput.**
The required data rate $D$ equals the product of sampling frequency, number of bits per sample, and number of simultaneous channels (single-lead ECG = 1 channel).

$$\begin{aligned}
D &= F_s \times W_{bits} \times N_{channels} \\
D &= 500\ \text{Hz} \times 12\ \text{bits} \times 1 \\
D &= 6{,}000\ \text{bps} = 6\ \text{kbps}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Add Protocol Overhead.**
BLE adds MAC headers, CRC, and ACK frames, typically inflating payload by ~ 60 %.

$$\begin{aligned}
D_{\text{air}} &= 6\ \text{kbps} \times 1.6 \\
D_{\text{air}} &= 9.6\ \text{kbps}
\end{aligned}$$

**Conclusion:** BLE's nominal **1 Mbps** link easily carries this 9.6 kbps stream with > 99 % margin.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Battery-Life Estimation of a Smart Inhaler

**Given Scenario:** A Bluetooth-enabled smart inhaler (used for asthma) uses a **CR2032 coin cell** of capacity **220 mAh** at 3 V. The device's average current draw, dominated by the BLE radio bursts, is measured at **1.8 mA** in active mode and **5 µA** in sleep mode. The radio is active for **10 seconds every 5 minutes** (i.e., 3.33 % duty cycle).

**Goal:** Estimate the operational battery life in days.

**Step 1 — Compute Time-Averaged Current.**
Using the duty-cycle weighting:

$$\begin{aligned}
I_{avg} &= I_{active} \cdot d + I_{sleep} \cdot (1 - d) \\
I_{avg} &= 1.8\ \text{mA} \times 0.0333 + 0.005\ \text{mA} \times 0.9667 \\
I_{avg} &= 0.0600\ \text{mA} + 0.00483\ \text{mA} \\
I_{avg} &= 0.0648\ \text{mA}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the Battery-Life Formula.**
For an ideal coin cell (no derating):

$$\begin{aligned}
T_{hours} &= \frac{C_{mAh}}{I_{avg}} \\
T_{hours} &= \frac{220\ \text{mAh}}{0.0648\ \text{mA}} \\
T_{hours} &= 3{,}395\ \text{hours}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Convert to Days.**

$$\begin{aligned}
T_{days} &= \frac{3{,}395}{24} \\
T_{days} &\approx 141.5\ \text{days} \approx 4.7\ \text{months}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Apply Real-World Derating.**
Coin cells lose ~ 30 % capacity at low temperatures and self-discharge ~ 2 % per year. A 70 % usable derating is industry standard.

$$\begin{aligned}
T_{days,\text{real}} &= 141.5 \times 0.70 \\
T_{days,\text{real}} &\approx 99\ \text{days} \approx 3.3\ \text{months}
\end{aligned}$$

**Conclusion:** A CR2032 suffices for ~ **3 months** of continuous smart-inhaler operation — a typical commercial benchmark (e.g., Propeller Health sensor).

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR) of a PPG Heart-Rate Sensor

**Given Scenario:** A reflective PPG (photoplethysmography) sensor on a smartwatch measures the reflected light intensity from the wrist. The peak-to-peak pulsatile signal amplitude is **2.5 mV**, while the RMS noise (motion artefact + shot noise + thermal noise) measures **0.4 mV**.

**Goal:** Compute the SNR in dB and judge whether the signal is clinically usable (clinical threshold = **20 dB**).

**Step 1 — Apply the Voltage SNR Definition.**

$$\begin{aligned}
SNR &= 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{V_{signal}}{V_{noise}}\right) \\
SNR &= 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{2.5\ \text{mV}}{0.4\ \text{mV}}\right) \\
SNR &= 20 \log_{10}(6.25) \\
SNR &= 20 \times 0.7959 \\
SNR &= 15.92\ \text{dB}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Compare to Clinical Threshold.**

$$\begin{aligned}
SNR_{calculated} = 15.92\ \text{dB} &< SNR_{clinical} = 20\ \text{dB}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Engineering Recommendation.**
The 4.08 dB shortfall must be recovered by:
- Adding a **band-pass filter (0.5 – 4 Hz)** to suppress motion artefact.
- Implementing **adaptive noise cancellation** using a tri-axial accelerometer reference.
- Increasing the **LED drive current** to boost the pulsatile signal by ~ 2.5×.

**Conclusion:** The raw sensor is **insufficient for clinical use**; signal-conditioning DSP is mandatory — a typical real-world example of why IoT healthcare design is as much a signal-processing problem as it is a connectivity problem.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> **Mermaid Compilation Note:** All node IDs are alphanumeric and prefixed; no reserved keywords are used as node IDs; all labels are clean uppercase text with no embedded markdown formatting.

### 4.1 End-to-End Healthcare IoT Architecture (Block-Level Functional Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph perceptionLayer["PERCEPTION LAYER  SENSING EDGE"]
        sensor1["ECG ELECTRODE PATCH"]
        sensor2["PPG SENSOR SPO2"]
        sensor3["MEMS ACCELEROMETER"]
        sensor4["GLUCOSE ENZYMATIC SENSOR"]
        sensor5["BODY TEMPERATURE RTD"]
        sensor6["INGESTIBLE SMART PILL"]
    end

    subgraph edgeLayer["EDGE LAYER  MICROCONTROLLER MCU"]
        mcuA["ADC SAMPLING 500 HZ"]
        mcuB["DIGITAL FILTER DSP"]
        mcuC["ON CHIP AI INFERENCE"]
        mcuD["AES 128 ENCRYPTION"]
    end

    subgraph networkLayer["NETWORK LAYER  WIRELESS TRANSPORT"]
        netA["BLE 2.4 GHZ 1 MBPS"]
        netB["ZIGBEE MESH 802.15.4"]
        netC["WIFI HOSPITAL AP"]
        netD["LORAWAN RURAL GATEWAY"]
        netE["NB IOT CELLULAR"]
    end

    subgraph processingLayer["PROCESSING LAYER  FOG AND CLOUD"]
        fogA["LOCAL GATEWAY RASPBERRY PI"]
        fogB["ANOMALY DETECTION ML MODEL"]
        cloudA["CLOUD EMR REPOSITORY"]
        cloudB["CLINICAL DECISION SUPPORT"]
    end

    subgraph applicationLayer["APPLICATION LAYER  END USERS"]
        appA["PATIENT MOBILE APP"]
        appB["CLINICIAN DASHBOARD"]
        appC["HOSPITAL ADMIN EHR"]
        appD["FAMILY CAREGIVER ALERTS"]
    end

    sensor1 --> mcuA
    sensor2 --> mcuA
    sensor3 --> mcuA
    sensor4 --> mcuA
    sensor5 --> mcuA
    sensor6 --> mcuA

    mcuA --> mcuB
    mcuB --> mcuC
    mcuC --> mcuD
    mcuD --> netA
    mcuD --> netB
    mcuD --> netC
    mcuD --> netD
    mcuD --> netE

    netA --> fogA
    netB --> fogA
    netC --> fogA
    netD --> fogA
    netE --> fogA

    fogA --> fogB
    fogB --> cloudA
    cloudA --> cloudB

    cloudB --> appA
    cloudB --> appB
    cloudB --> appC
    cloudB --> appD
```

### 4.2 Internal Anatomy of a Generic Healthcare IoT Node

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph node["IOT HEALTHCARE NODE INTERNAL BLOCKS"]
        biosig["BIOSIGNAL SOURCE HUMAN BODY"]
        tranducer["TRANSDUCER ELECTROCHEMICAL OPTICAL MECHANICAL"]
        analogFront["ANALOG FRONT END AMPLIFIER FILTER"]
        adc["ADC 12 TO 16 BIT"]
        mcu["MCU NORDIC NRF STM32"]
        crypto["CRYPTO ENGINE AES 128"]
        radio["RADIO BLE ZIGBEE LORA"]
        antenna["ANTENNA PCB TRACE"]
        power["POWER MANAGEMENT"]
        battery["BATTERY 220 MAH COIN CELL"]
        harv["ENERGY HARVESTING THERMAL PIEZO"]
    end

    biosig --> tranducer
    tranducer --> analogFront
    analogFront --> adc
    adc --> mcu
    mcu --> crypto
    crypto --> radio
    radio --> antenna

    power --> mcu
    power --> radio
    power --> adc
    battery --> power
    harv --> power
```

### 4.3 Closed-Loop Remote Patient Monitoring (RPM) Data Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start(["PATIENT AT HOME"]) --> wear["WEARABLE PATCH COLLECTS VITALS"]
    wear --> enc["EDGE ENCRYPTS AND BUFFERS"]
    enc --> tx["TRANSMIT VIA BLE TO PHONE"]
    tx --> cell["PHONE FORWARDS VIA 4G 5G TO CLOUD"]
    cell --> ai["CLOUD AI ANALYSES TREND"]
    ai --> decision{"ANOMALY DETECTED?"}
    decision -- NO --> store["STORE IN EHR"]
    decision -- YES --> alert["GENERATE ALERT"]
    alert --> nurse(["CARE COORDINATOR NURSE"])
    alert --> telemed(["INITIATE TELEMEDICINE VIDEO CALL"])
    nurse --> doc(["ONCALL PHYSICIAN REVIEWS"])
    telemed --> doc
    doc --> intervene(["ADJUST MEDICATION OR DISPATCH AMBULANCE"])
    intervene --> start
    store --> start
```

> **Reading the Diagram:** The closed loop is what makes healthcare IoT *transformative* — it converts episodic care into **continuous, predictive, and preventive** care.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Q1. Define the Internet of Things (IoT) in the context of healthcare. List any four examples of medical IoT devices.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The Internet of Things (IoT) in healthcare is a network of interconnected medical devices, sensors, and software systems that collect, transmit, and analyze patient health data to enable remote monitoring, diagnosis, and treatment. **(1 mark)**

Four examples: **(2 marks — ½ mark each)**
1. **Smartwatch with ECG** (e.g., Apple Watch Series) — detects atrial fibrillation.
2. **Continuous Glucose Monitor (CGM)** (e.g., Dexcom G7) — tracks blood sugar in diabetics.
3. **Implantable Pacemaker** (e.g., Medtronic Azure) — monitors and regulates heart rhythm.
4. **Smart Inhaler** (e.g., Propeller Health) — tracks asthma medication adherence.

---

> **[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**

**Q2. Differentiate between BLE, ZigBee, and LoRaWAN for healthcare IoT. Mention one medical use-case of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Protocol | Range | Power | Data Rate | Healthcare Use-Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **BLE** | 10 m | Very Low | 1 Mbps | **Wearable ECG patch** (1 mark) |
| **ZigBee** | 100 m | Low | 250 kbps | **In-hospital mesh for patient tracking** (1 mark) |
| **LoRaWAN** | 2–10 km | Very Low | 50 kbps | **Rural elderly fall-detection pendant** (1 mark) |

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU ESE Module-1 Internal Choice: Answer ANY ONE full question (a + b).**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]*

**(a) Explain the four-layer architecture of a healthcare IoT system. For each layer, give one specific example of a device, protocol, or function. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Layer 1 — Perception (Sensor) Layer: (2 marks)**
This is the physical layer that interfaces with the human body. It includes biomedical sensors that convert physiological phenomena (heartbeat, temperature, glucose concentration) into electrical signals.
*Example:* A **PPG sensor** in a smartwatch that uses green LEDs and photodiodes to measure SpO₂.

**Layer 2 — Network (Communication) Layer: (2 marks)**
This layer transports the digitized data from the sensor to a processing node. It must balance range, power, and bandwidth.
*Example:* **Bluetooth Low Energy (BLE)** carrying the 9.6 kbps ECG stream from a chest patch to a smartphone.

**Layer 3 — Processing (Middleware) Layer: (2 marks)**
This layer performs aggregation, storage, analytics, and AI inference. It can be split into edge, fog, and cloud tiers.
*Example:* A **cloud-based ML model** (e.g., a CNN trained on arrhythmia data) that flags abnormal beats.

**Layer 4 — Application Layer: (1 mark)**
This layer delivers the value to the end user via dashboards, alerts, and decision-support tools.
*Example:* A **clinician dashboard** that visualizes real-time vitals of 50 ICU patients.

---

**(b) A wearable pulse oximeter measures SpO₂ at 100 Hz using 16-bit samples. Compute (i) the minimum sampling rate per Nyquist, (ii) the throughput in kbps, and (iii) the expected battery life if a 150 mAh battery powers a device that draws 0.05 mA average current. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Minimum Sampling Rate — Nyquist Check:** (2 marks)
The problem *states* the sensor already operates at 100 Hz, meaning the maximum meaningful frequency in the SpO₂ plethysmographic waveform is up to ~ 50 Hz (peripheral pulse is rich in < 5 Hz, but motion artefact and harmonics require higher sampling).

$$\begin{aligned}
F_{s,\min} &= 2 \times F_{max} = 2 \times 50 = 100\ \text{Hz}
\end{aligned}$$

The device is operating **exactly at Nyquist**, which is marginal. **Recommendation:** In practice, **250 Hz** is used for SpO₂ to capture motion artefacts.

**(ii) Throughput Calculation:** (2 marks)

$$\begin{aligned}
D &= F_s \times W_{bits} \\
D &= 100\ \text{Hz} \times 16\ \text{bits} \\
D &= 1{,}600\ \text{bps} = 1.6\ \text{kbps}
\end{aligned}$$

**(iii) Battery Life Calculation:** (3 marks)

$$\begin{aligned}
T_{hours} &= \frac{C_{mAh}}{I_{avg}} \\
T_{hours} &= \frac{150\ \text{mAh}}{0.05\ \text{mA}} \\
T_{hours} &= 3{,}000\ \text{hours} \\
T_{days} &= \frac{3{,}000}{24} = 125\ \text{days} \approx 4.1\ \text{months}
\end{aligned}$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Modified]*

**(a) Discuss the major challenges of deploying IoT devices in healthcare. Mention at least five challenges. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Data Security & Privacy (HIPAA/GDPR Compliance):** Patient health data is highly sensitive; any breach can be life-threatening (e.g., a hacked insulin pump delivers fatal doses). Encryption (AES-128/256) and mutual authentication are mandatory. **(2 marks)**

2. **Interoperability:** Devices from different vendors must speak the same language. Standards like **HL7 FHIR, IEEE 11073, and Continua Design Guidelines** are still maturing. **(1 mark)**

3. **Power Management:** Continuous monitoring demands energy, but miniaturization limits battery size. Solutions include energy harvesting (body-heat, piezoelectric) and ultra-low-power MCUs. **(1 mark)**

4. **Reliability & Latency:** Medical decisions depend on real-time, accurate data. Latency must be **< 200 ms**; false alarms cause "alarm fatigue" in clinical staff. **(1 mark)**

5. **Data Validation & Clinical Accuracy:** Consumer wearables may not match clinical gold standards (FDA Class II). The 15.92 dB PPG example shows consumer accuracy can fall short of the 20 dB clinical threshold. **(1 mark)**

6. **Regulatory Hurdles & Ethical Concerns:** FDA / CDSCO approval, liability in case of device failure, and patient consent for continuous monitoring. **(1 mark)**

---

**(b) With a neat block diagram, describe the closed-loop operation of a Remote Patient Monitoring (RPM) system for a chronic heart-failure patient. Highlight the role of AI in the loop. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Block Diagram (textual — see Section 4.3 for the full Mermaid schematic):**
Patient → Wearable ECG + Weight Scale → BLE → Smartphone → 4G/5G → Cloud → AI Analytics Engine → Decision (Normal / Abnormal) → Alert → Nurse / Physician → Intervention → Back to Patient.

**Step-by-step explanation:** (5 marks for narrative + 2 marks for AI discussion)

1. **Sensing (1 mark):** The patient wears an **ECG patch** (500 Hz, 12-bit) and a **smart weight scale** (sudden weight gain indicates fluid retention, a key heart-failure precursor).
2. **Transmission (1 mark):** Data is encrypted (AES-128) and sent via **BLE** to the patient's phone, which forwards it via **4G/5G** to the hospital cloud.
3. **AI Analytics (2 marks):** A **Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM)** recurrent neural network analyses the time-series ECG and weight data, predicting decompensation **48–72 hours before symptoms appear**.
4. **Alert & Intervention (1 mark):** On detecting risk, the system **alerts a care-coordinator nurse**, who conducts a **telemedicine video call** with the patient, adjusts diuretic dosage, and prevents hospitalization.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mark Loss Zones**
> 1. **Confusing BLE with "Bluetooth Classic":** BLE is *not* the same as classic Bluetooth. Mark deduction ~ 1 mark.
> 2. **Forgetting Protocol Overhead:** Bare throughput = 6 kbps does NOT mean BLE link uses only 6 kbps. Account for ~ 60 % MAC overhead. Lose ½ mark.
> 3. **Skipping Unit Conversions:** Writing battery life as "3000" without converting to days. Lose ½ mark.
> 4. **No mention of FDA / Clinical Threshold in SNR problems:** Always compare computed SNR to the **20 dB clinical benchmark**. Lose 1 mark if skipped.
> 5. **Treating "Wearables" and "Implantables" as synonyms:** They are different — wearables are external, implantables are subcutaneous/ingestible. Lose 1 mark.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Recap — Save This Page for Last-Minute Revision**

- ✅ **Definition:** IoT in healthcare = interconnected sensors + devices + network + cloud + app, working as a **closed-loop CPS**.
- ✅ **Four Layers:** **Perception → Network → Processing → Application** (sometimes extended with a *Business* layer as the 5th).
- ✅ **Device Categories:** Wearables, Implantables, Ambient, Mobile/Handheld.
- ✅ **Top 6 Communication Protocols:** BLE, ZigBee, Wi-Fi, LoRaWAN, NB-IoT, NFC — and their trade-off matrix.
- ✅ **Critical Constants to Memorize:**
   - Nyquist: $F_s \geq 2 F_{max}$
   - ECG diagnostic sampling: **500 Hz**, 12 bits
   - Clinical SNR threshold: **20 dB**
   - Maximum acceptable alarm latency: **< 200 ms**
   - Typical BLE range: **10 m**, throughput **1 Mbps** (PHY), ~ **260 kbps** effective
- ✅ **Battery-Life Formula:** $T_{hours} = C_{mAh} / I_{avg}$ → always convert to days in the answer.
- ✅ **SNR Formula:** $SNR_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}(V_s / V_n)$.
- ✅ **Wheatstone-Bridge Biosensor:** $V_{out} = V_{ex} \cdot \Delta R / (4R)$.
- ✅ **Healthcare IoT Trilemma:** Size ⇄ Battery ⇄ Intelligence — pick any two.
- ✅ **Top 5 Challenges:** Security, Interoperability, Power, Reliability, Clinical Accuracy.
- ✅ **Closed-Loop RPM Flow:** Sensor → Encrypt → Transmit → Cloud AI → Decision → Alert → Clinician → Patient.
- ✅ **Regulatory Keywords:** FDA Class II, HIPAA, GDPR, IEC 60601, IEEE 11073, HL7 FHIR, Continua.
- ✅ **Real-World Devices to Quote in Answers:** Apple Watch ECG, Dexcom CGM, Medtronic Pacemaker, Propeller Smart Inhaler, AliveCor KardiaMobile.
- ✅ **Most-Asked KTU Question Pattern:** "Calculate throughput / battery life given a sensor spec" — always show *every* algebraic step.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Data Sharing and Secondary Use of Health Data
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEDAT751 - COMPUTATIONAL HEALTH INFORMATICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Data Sharing and Secondary Use of Health Data

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Data Sharing in Healthcare** is the formalized, governed, and technically mediated process of transmitting, exposing, or granting controlled access to clinical, administrative, genomic, and patient-reported health information to authorized internal and external stakeholders beyond the immediate patient–provider encounter.

**Secondary Use of Health Data (SUHD)** is defined by the *Office of the National Coordinator for Health IT (ONC, USA)* and adopted by KTU's PEDAT751 framework as:

> *"The use of health data, originally collected during routine clinical care or administrative processes, for purposes other than the direct provision of healthcare to the individual patient — encompassing research, public health surveillance, policy formulation, quality measurement, commercial analytics, and educational applications."*

The complementary term — **Primary Use** — refers to data used by clinicians and patients at the point-of-care to inform treatment decisions.

> [!IMPORTANT] **KTU 2024 Highlight — The "Purpose Limitation Principle"**
> Per KTU Module 1 (NEP 2020 Outcome-Based Education), every datum in an EHR carries an *implicit original purpose*. Any deviation from this purpose transitions the use-case from **Primary → Secondary**, which legally and ethically requires a fresh governance evaluation under frameworks such as **HIPAA**, **GDPR**, or India's proposed **DISHA Act**.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Plain English Intuition)

Imagine a hospital is like a **large library**, and each patient's medical record is a **unique book** written in a language called "EHR."

- **Primary Use (Reading the book):** A doctor borrows the book to treat the patient — this is its *original purpose*.
- **Secondary Use (Repurposing the book):** The librarian notices patterns across thousands of books:
  - A **researcher** wants to study which diabetes medicines work best (Research).
  - The **government** wants to track a flu outbreak (Public Health Surveillance).
  - A **policy maker** wants to know how many beds are needed (Planning).
  - A **pharmaceutical company** wants to find candidates for a clinical trial (Commercial).

The library must now decide: *Can the books be shared? With whom? Under what rules? How do we protect the identities of the readers (patients)?* — This is **Data Sharing Governance**.

> [!NOTE] **Intuitive Memory Hook**
> Think of health data as **water from a tap (Primary Use)**. The same water can be **collected, filtered, bottled, and redistributed** (Secondary Use). The *filtration process* is **de-identification**, the *bottling labels* are **metadata**, and the *safety regulators* are **IRBs and ethics committees**.

---

### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Lexical Anchors

The following constants and standards are repeatedly cited in KTU Module 1 and global health informatics literature:

| Anchor Term | Standard / Constant | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| HIPAA Safe Harbor | **18 identifier categories** removed | Statutory de-identification standard (US) |
| HIPAA Expert Determination | Statistical certification by qualified expert | Alternative de-identification method |
| GDPR Article 9 | "Special Category" personal data | EU's strictest data class |
| OMOP CDM | v5.4 (OHDSI standard) | Common Data Model for secondary research |
| k-Anonymity Threshold | **k = 5** (commonly) | Minimum equivalence class size |
| Differential Privacy ε | ε ≤ **1.0** (strong), ≤ **10.0** (weak) | Privacy budget parameter |
| INDICATE Consortium | 10 ethical principles (2013) | Framework for secondary data ethics |

---

### 1.4 Categories of Secondary Use (Saeed & Mark, *JAMIA* Taxonomy)

Six dominant categories, each examined in detail in subsequent sections:

1. **Clinical & Translational Research** — cohort discovery, drug repurposing.
2. **Public Health Surveillance** — disease registries, outbreak detection.
3. **Quality & Safety Measurement** — hospital readmission benchmarking.
4. **Health Policy & Planning** — resource allocation, insurance design.
5. **Commercial / Industry Use** — pharma R&D, insurance risk modeling.
6. **Education & Training** — synthetic case generation, ML training sets.

> [!NOTE] **Pedagogical Hook for KTU Students**
> Whenever asked "What is secondary use?" in an exam, always anchor your answer in the **three-legged stool**: *(1) Data Origin, (2) New Purpose, (3) Governance Re-evaluation*. This simple triad scores full marks under KTU's CO1 (Understanding) mapping.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Theoretical Stack: A Layered Model

Health data sharing operates across **four interacting layers**, each with its own theoretical scaffolding:

#### **Layer 1 — Legal & Regulatory Layer**
- **HIPAA (1996, US)** — Privacy Rule, Security Rule, Breach Notification.
- **GDPR (2018, EU)** — Article 6 (lawful basis), Article 9 (special categories), Article 17 (right to erasure).
- **India's DISHA (proposed 2018)** — Digital Information Security in Healthcare Act.
- **The principle of "minimum necessary"** — disclose only the data fields essential to the secondary purpose.

#### **Layer 2 — Ethical Layer**
- **Autonomy** — patient consent.
- **Beneficence** — does the secondary use produce net good?
- **Non-maleficence** — re-identification harm, group stigmatization.
- **Justice** — equitable distribution of research benefits and burdens.
- **Solidarity** — particularly emphasized in genomics (e.g., UK Biobank model).

#### **Layer 3 — Technical Layer**
- **De-identification algorithms** (Safe Harbor, k-anonymity, l-diversity, t-closeness).
- **Privacy-Enhancing Technologies (PETs)** — differential privacy, homomorphic encryption, federated learning, secure multi-party computation (SMPC).
- **Common Data Models (CDM)** — OMOP, PCORnet, i2b2/ACT, Sentinel.

#### **Layer 4 — Governance & Operational Layer**
- Data Use Agreements (DUA), Business Associate Agreements (BAA).
- Institutional Review Boards (IRB) / Ethics Committees.
- Data Access Committees (DAC) — exemplified by *dbGaP*, *UK Biobank*.
- Trusted Execution Environments (TEE) and Federated Query Systems.

---

### 2.2 Why Data Sharing is Non-Trivial — The Five Tensions

| Tension | Description | KTU Theoretical Anchor |
|---|---|---|
| **Privacy vs. Utility** | More anonymization → less analytical value | Information-theoretic tradeoff |
| **Open Science vs. Commercial IP** | Public benefit vs. proprietary datasets | Bayh-Dole Act, NIH Data Management Policy |
| **Individual vs. Group Rights** | Patient consent vs. community benefit (e.g., Indigenous genomics) | CARE Principles for Indigenous Data Governance |
| **Speed vs. Governance** | Pandemic urgency vs. regulatory due process | COVID-19 *EUA* (Emergency Use Authorization) precedent |
| **Local vs. Global Standards** | National laws vs. cross-border research consortia | GDPR extraterritoriality (Article 3) |

---

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — Secondary Use of Health Data

> [!NOTE] The following equations form the **analytical backbone** of Module 1 derivations and Part B numerical questions.

$$
H(X) \;=\; -\sum_{i=1}^{n} p(x_i) \, \log_2 p(x_i)
$$

$$
\mathcal{P}_{\text{re-id}} \;=\; \Pr[\text{identity} \mid \text{quasi-identifiers}]
$$

$$
\Pr[M(D) \in S] \;\leq\; e^{\varepsilon} \cdot \Pr[M(D') \in S]
$$

$$
k\text{-anonymity constraint:} \quad \forall r \in T,\; \vert \{ r' \in T \mid r' \equiv_k r \} \vert \;\geq\; k
$$

$$
I(X;Y) \;=\; H(X) - H(X \mid Y) \;=\; H(Y) - H(Y \mid X)
$$

$$
n \;=\; \frac{Z^{2} \cdot p(1-p)}{d^{2}}
$$

$$
\text{Power} \;=\; 1 - \beta \;=\; \Phi\!\left( \frac{\vert \mu_1 - \mu_2 \vert \sqrt{n}}{\sigma} - Z_{1-\alpha/2} \right)
$$

$$
\text{F1} \;=\; 2 \cdot \frac{\text{Precision} \cdot \text{Recall}}{\text{Precision} + \text{Recall}}
$$

$$
\text{PPV} \;=\; \frac{\text{TP}}{\text{TP} + \text{FP}} \qquad\qquad \text{NPV} \;=\; \frac{\text{TN}}{\text{TN} + \text{FN}}
$$

#### **Symbol Legend & Units**

| Symbol | Meaning | Typical Value / Unit |
|---|---|---|
| $H(X)$ | Shannon entropy of attribute X | bits |
| $p(x_i)$ | Probability mass of value $x_i$ | dimensionless, $\in [0,1]$ |
| $\mathcal{P}_{\text{re-id}}$ | Re-identification risk | dimensionless, target $\leq 0.05$ |
| $\varepsilon$ | Differential privacy budget | dimensionless, $\in (0, 10]$ |
| $M(\cdot)$ | Randomized query mechanism | function |
| $D, D'$ | Adjacent datasets (differ by 1 record) | records |
| $k$ | Anonymity parameter | integer, $\geq 2$ |
| $I(X;Y)$ | Mutual information | bits |
| $n$ | Required sample size | records |
| $Z$ | Z-score (1.96 for 95% CI) | dimensionless |
| $d$ | Margin of error | proportion |
| $\Phi$ | Standard normal CDF | dimensionless |

> [!IMPORTANT] **Engineer's Field Insight**
> In real hospital biobanks, the *Privacy Budget* $\varepsilon$ is treated like a *financial budget*: once exhausted by repeated queries, the dataset is "locked" to prevent leakage. This mirrors differential privacy in Google's **RAPPOR** and Apple's iOS telemetry systems.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> **Pedagogical Note:** All derivations below are *exhaustive*; no algebraic step is skipped. These three derivations correspond to the highest-weight (14-mark) Part B questions under KTU's ESE pattern.

---

### 3.1 Derivation I — Information Entropy of a Diagnostic Code Distribution

**Premise:** A hospital's EHR contains $N$ discharge records. Each record carries a primary ICD-10 diagnosis drawn from a discrete alphabet of $n$ codes with empirical frequencies $p_1, p_2, \dots, p_n$.

**Step 1 — Axiomatic Foundation.**

Shannon's entropy is uniquely defined (up to a constant base) by three axioms:
1. Continuity in $p_i$.
2. Maximum when the distribution is uniform.
3. Recursive decomposition: $H(p_1, \dots, p_n) = H(p_1 + p_2, p_3, \dots, p_n) + (p_1 + p_2) H\!\left(\frac{p_1}{p_1 + p_2}, \frac{p_2}{p_1 + p_2}\right)$.

**Step 2 — Closed-Form Expression.**

Combining these axioms yields:

$$
H(X) \;=\; -K \sum_{i=1}^{n} p(x_i) \, \log_b p(x_i)
$$

We choose $K = 1$, $b = 2$, so the unit becomes **bits**.

**Step 3 — Worked Numerical Example.**

Consider four ICD-10 codes with the following empirical frequencies across $N = 1000$ records:

| ICD-10 Code | Count | Probability $p_i$ |
|---|---|---|
| E11.9 (Type 2 DM) | 480 | 0.480 |
| I10 (Essential HTN) | 320 | 0.320 |
| J45.909 (Asthma) | 150 | 0.150 |
| M54.5 (Low back pain) | 50 | 0.050 |

**Step 4 — Plug Into the Formula.**

$$
H(X) \;=\; -\!\left[ 0.480 \log_2 0.480 + 0.320 \log_2 0.320 + 0.150 \log_2 0.150 + 0.050 \log_2 0.050 \right]
$$

**Step 5 — Component Evaluation.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
0.480 \log_2 0.480 &= 0.480 \times (-1.0589) &= -0.5083 \\
0.320 \log_2 0.320 &= 0.320 \times (-1.6439) &= -0.5261 \\
0.150 \log_2 0.150 &= 0.150 \times (-2.7370) &= -0.4105 \\
0.050 \log_2 0.050 &= 0.050 \times (-4.3219) &= -0.2161
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Summation and Sign Flip.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
H(X) &= -\, [\, (-0.5083) + (-0.5261) + (-0.4105) + (-0.2161) \,] \\
&= -\, [\, -1.6610 \,] \\
&= 1.6610 \text{ bits}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 7 — Interpretation.**

The maximum possible entropy is $\log_2 4 = 2.000$ bits (uniform distribution). The observed entropy **1.6610 bits** indicates the diagnosis distribution is moderately concentrated — primarily in diabetes and hypertension. This metric is foundational for **information loss quantification** during de-identification.

---

### 3.2 Derivation II — Re-identification Risk via Bayes' Theorem

**Premise:** A released dataset contains three quasi-identifiers: 3-digit ZIP code, gender, and birth year. We want the probability that an attacker uniquely identifies a known individual.

**Step 1 — Bayes' Theorem Statement.**

$$
\Pr[A \mid B] \;=\; \frac{\Pr[B \mid A] \cdot \Pr[A]}{\Pr[B]}
$$

**Step 2 — Mapping to Re-identification.**

Let:
- $A$ = target individual is in the dataset.
- $B$ = attacker's external knowledge matches the quasi-identifier tuple.

Then:

$$
\mathcal{P}_{\text{re-id}} \;=\; \Pr[A \mid B] \;=\; \frac{\Pr[B \mid A] \cdot \Pr[A]}{\Pr[B \mid A]\Pr[A] + \Pr[B \mid A^c]\Pr[A^c]}
$$

**Step 3 — Numerical Substitution.**

Assume:
- $\Pr[A] = 1/100{,}000$ (population-level prior; one record per capita in dataset).
- $\Pr[B \mid A] = 0.85$ (linkage accuracy of attacker).
- $\Pr[B \mid A^c] = 0.0001$ (background match rate).

**Step 4 — Numerator.**

$$
\Pr[B \mid A] \cdot \Pr[A] \;=\; 0.85 \times \frac{1}{100{,}000} \;=\; 8.5 \times 10^{-6}
$$

**Step 5 — Denominator.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Denom} &= (0.85)(10^{-5}) + (0.0001)(1 - 10^{-5}) \\
&= 8.5 \times 10^{-6} + 0.0001 \times 0.99999 \\
&= 8.5 \times 10^{-6} + 9.9999 \times 10^{-5} \\
&= 1.08499 \times 10^{-4}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Final Calculation.**

$$
\mathcal{P}_{\text{re-id}} \;=\; \frac{8.5 \times 10^{-6}}{1.08499 \times 10^{-4}} \;\approx\; 0.0783
$$

**Step 7 — Engineering Interpretation.**

A risk of **7.83%** exceeds HIPAA's "very small risk" threshold (typically interpreted as $< 6\%$ per the *Sweeney Latanya* studies). Therefore, this quasi-identifier combination **must be further generalized** (e.g., collapse ZIP to 2-digit, or birth year to decade).

---

### 3.3 Derivation III — Sample Size for a Secondary-Use Cohort Study

**Premise:** A researcher plans a retrospective cohort study using a shared de-identified dataset. The expected prevalence difference between exposed and unexposed groups is $d = 0.10$, with confidence level 95% and power 80%.

**Step 1 — Recall the Standard Formula.**

$$
n \;=\; \frac{Z^{2} \cdot p(1-p)}{d^{2}}
$$

**Step 2 — Critical Values.**

- $Z = 1.96$ (95% confidence, two-tailed).
- $p$ is the expected proportion; we use $p = 0.5$ (most conservative).
- $d = 0.10$.

**Step 3 — Substitution.**

$$
n \;=\; \frac{(1.96)^{2} \cdot 0.5 \cdot 0.5}{(0.10)^{2}}
$$

**Step 4 — Component Evaluation.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
(1.96)^2 &= 3.8416 \\
0.5 \times 0.5 &= 0.25 \\
(0.10)^2 &= 0.01
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Computation.**

$$
n \;=\; \frac{3.8416 \times 0.25}{0.01} \;=\; \frac{0.9604}{0.01} \;=\; 96.04
$$

**Step 6 — Round Up and Adjust for Attrition.**

Apply a 20% attrition buffer:

$$
n_{\text{final}} \;=\; \lceil 96.04 \rceil \times 1.20 \;\approx\; 116 \text{ records per arm}
$$

**Step 7 — Engineering Conclusion.**

The requesting biobank must provide at least **232 total records** to support this secondary analysis. This sample size calculation directly informs the **dataset filtering logic** in OMOP CDM cohort definitions.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Mapping Laws to Techniques

| Law / Standard | De-id Method Mandated | Risk Threshold | KTU Exam Reference |
|---|---|---|---|
| HIPAA Safe Harbor (US) | Remove 18 identifiers | "Very small" | Module 1, Q4 |
| HIPAA Expert Determination | Statistical certification | "Very small" | Module 1, Q3 |
| GDPR (EU) | Pseudonymization + lawful basis | Risk-based DPIA | Module 1, Q5 |
| INDICATE (Ethics) | Consent + solidarity | Contextual | Module 1, Q2 |
| DISHA (India, proposed) | ABDM-compliant anonymization | To be defined | Module 1, Q6 |
| Common Rule (45 CFR 46) | IRB review, broad consent | Risk-based | Module 1, Q2 |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram I — Primary → Secondary Use Data Flow

The following block diagram captures the canonical transformation from a single patient's clinical encounter to multiple downstream secondary use cases.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[PATIENT CLINICAL ENCOUNTER] --> B[EHR DATA GENERATION]
    B --> C[PRIMARY USE BY CLINICIAN]
    B --> D[DATA WAREHOUSE ETL]
    D --> E[DE IDENTIFICATION ENGINE]
    E --> F[SAFE HARBOR METHOD]
    E --> G[EXPERT DETERMINATION]
    E --> H[DIFFERENTIAL PRIVACY LAYER]
    F --> I[SECONDARY USE DATASET]
    G --> I
    H --> I
    I --> J[CLINICAL RESEARCH]
    I --> K[PUBLIC HEALTH SURVEILLANCE]
    I --> L[QUALITY MEASUREMENT]
    I --> M[POLICY AND PLANNING]
    I --> N[COMMERCIAL ANALYTICS]
    I --> O[EDUCATION AND TRAINING]
```

---

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram II — OHDSI Federated Network Architecture

This schematic illustrates the *Observational Health Data Sciences and Informatics* (OHDSI) network — the gold standard for distributed secondary use research.

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph DATA_PARTNERS
        P1[HOSPITAL A OMOP CDM]
        P2[HOSPITAL B OMOP CDM]
        P3[INSURANCE CLAIMS DB]
        P4[GOVERNMENT REGISTRY]
    end

    subgraph COORDINATING_CENTER
        Q1[STUDY COORDINATOR]
        Q2[STUDY PACKAGE SQL]
        Q3[AGGREGATED RESULTS DATABASE]
    end

    P1 -->|RUN LOCALLY| Q2
    P2 -->|RUN LOCALLY| Q2
    P3 -->|RUN LOCALLY| Q2
    P4 -->|RUN LOCALLY| Q2
    Q2 --> Q1
    Q1 --> Q3
    Q3 --> R[PUBLICATION GRADE EVIDENCE]
```

---

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram III — Informed Consent Decision Tree

A governance-level flowchart for selecting the appropriate consent regime under the **Common Rule** and **GDPR Article 6/9**.

```mermaid
graph TD
    S[SECONDARY USE REQUEST] --> T{IS THE DATA FULLY ANONYMIZED}
    T -->|YES| U[NO CONSENT REQUIRED]
    T -->|NO| V{IS BROAD CONSENT IN PLACE}
    V -->|YES| W[PROCEED WITH IRB REVIEW]
    V -->|NO| X{CAN RE CONTACT BE DONE}
    X -->|YES| Y[SEEK SPECIFIC CONSENT]
    X -->|NO| Z[APPLY WAIVER OF CONSENT VIA IRB]
```

> [!NOTE] **Architecture Insight**
> Notice the *federated* execution in Diagram II: study code travels **to** the data, not the reverse. This is the architectural inversion that made OHDSI's COVID-19 studies (~500 million patient records, 2020–2022) both rapid and privacy-preserving.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1.** *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]*  
Define **secondary use of health data**. List any **four** categories with one example each.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
