Statistical / Morphological Features** — derived from the distribution, shape, or texture of the data. ### Stage 4 — Feature Representation (Encoding for ML) - Numerical features are **normalized** to a common scale. - Categorical features (e.g., blood group, gender) are **encoded** numerically. - High-dimensional feature vectors may be projected into lower-dimensional space via **dimensionality reduction**. ## 2.2 Comprehensive KTU Formula Sheet > [!NOTE] > The following table contains every equation you must know for this topic. Memorize the symbol, the formula, and the unit. | **Feature / Metric** | **Mathematical Formula** | **Domain of Use** | **Typical Unit** | |---|---|---|---| | Mean Amplitude | $\bar{x} = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i$ | ECG, EEG, EMG signals | mV or $\mu$V | | Standard Deviation | $\sigma = \sqrt{\frac{1}{N-1} \sum_{i=1}^{N} (x_i - \bar{x})^2}$ | All signal types | Same as signal | | Variance | $\sigma^2 = \frac{1}{N-1} \sum_{i=1}^{N} (x_i - \bar{x})^2$ | Signal variability | (signal)$^2$ | | Root Mean Square (RMS) | $x_{rms} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i^2}$ | EMG power, ECG energy | mV | | Signal Energy | $E = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \vert x_i \vert^2$ | Wavelet sub-bands | (mV)$^2$ | | Zero-Crossing Rate | $ZCR = \frac{1}{2N} \sum_{i=1}^{N-1} \vert \text{sgn}(x_{i+1}) - \text{sgn}(x_i) \vert$ | EEG seizure detection | count/sample | | Signal-to-Noise Ratio | $SNR_{dB} = 10 \log_{10} \frac{P_{signal}}{P_{noise}}$ | Quality of medical signal | **dB** | | Power Spectral Density (PSD) | $PSD(f) = \lim_{T \to \infty} \frac{\vert X_T(f) \vert^2}{T}$ | Frequency analysis of signals | V$^2$/Hz | | Spectral Entropy | $H = - \sum_{f} p(f) \log_2 p(f)$ | EEG complexity | bits | | TF-IDF Weight | $w_{t,d} = tf_{t,d} \cdot \log \frac{N}{df_t}$ | Clinical NLP, text mining | dimensionless | | Min-Max Normalization | $x'_{i} = \frac{x_i - x_{min}}{x_{max} - x_{min}}$ | Scale normalization | dimensionless [0,1] | | Z-Score Standardization | $x'_{i} = \frac{x_i - \bar{x}}{\sigma}$ | Statistical scaling | dimensionless | | Euclidean Distance | $d_E(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y}) = \sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{d} (x_i - y_i)^2}$ | KNN, clustering in feature space | same as features | | Manhattan Distance | $d_M(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y}) = \sum_{i=1}^{d} \vert x_i - y_i \vert$ | KNN, sparse data | same as features | | Cosine Similarity | $\cos(\theta) = \frac{\mathbf{x} \cdot \mathbf{y}}{\Vert \mathbf{x} \Vert \cdot \Vert \mathbf{y} \Vert}$ | Document similarity, gene vectors | [-1, +1] | | Mahalanobis Distance | $d_M(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y}) = \sqrt{(\mathbf{x} - \mathbf{y})^T S^{-1} (\mathbf{x} - \mathbf{y})}$ | Correlated medical features | scale-invariant | ## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility - **ECG-based Arrhythmia Detection:** 12–18 statistical and morphological features (RR interval, QRS width, P-wave amplitude) are extracted per heartbeat and fed to an SVM or Random Forest classifier. The MIT-BIH Arrhythmia Database benchmark relies entirely on this pipeline. - **Brain-Computer Interfaces (BCI):** EEG signals are decomposed into delta, theta, alpha, beta, and gamma bands via Fourier transform; the **band power** in each band serves as features for motor-imagery classification. - **Radiomics in Oncology:** Hundreds of texture features (GLCM, GLRLM, GLSZM) are extracted from CT/MRI tumor regions to predict malignancy, treatment response, and patient survival. - **Clinical NLP:** Patient discharge notes are converted to TF-IDF vectors and used to predict hospital readmission risk. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Rule of Thumb (High-Yield)** — Always normalize your feature vectors before computing distances or feeding them to gradient-based learners. Failure to scale causes features with larger numerical ranges to dominate the learning algorithm artificially. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: TF-IDF Weighting for Clinical Text The TF-IDF (Term Frequency–Inverse Document Frequency) is a cornerstone representation for clinical free-text (e.g., discharge summaries, radiology reports). It is a **favourite KTU 14-mark derivation**. ### Step 1 — Define Term Frequency (TF) Term Frequency measures how often a medical term $t$ appears in a single clinical document $d$. $$ tf(t, d) = \frac{f_{t,d}}{\sum_{t' \in d} f_{t',d}} $$ **Explanation:** $f_{t,d}$ is the raw count of term $t$ in document $d$. Dividing by the total number of words in the document normalizes for document length. A long radiology report and a short prescription should not be compared unfairly. ### Step 2 — Define Document Frequency (DF) Document Frequency counts in how many documents of the clinical corpus a term $t$ appears. $$ df(t) = \sum_{d \in D} \mathbf{1}\{t \in d\} $$ **Explanation:** $\mathbf{1}\{t \in d\}$ is an indicator function equal to 1 if term $t$ appears in document $d$, else 0. Summing over all documents in corpus $D$ gives the count. ### Step 3 — Define Inverse Document Frequency (IDF) IDF down-weights terms that appear in many documents (e.g., the word "patient") and up-weights rare but informative terms (e.g., "tachycardia", "metastasis"). $$ idf(t) = \log \frac{N}{df(t)} $$ **Explanation:** $N$ is the total number of documents in the corpus. If a term appears in *all* $N$ documents, $idf = 0$ (uninformative). If it appears in only 1, $idf = \log N$ (highly informative). ### Step 4 — Combine to Form TF-IDF $$ w_{t,d} = tf(t,d) \times idf(t) = tf(t,d) \cdot \log \frac{N}{df(t)} $$ **Explanation:** The product captures both the local importance (within one document) and the global rarity (across the corpus). The result is a numerical weight that represents how discriminative a clinical term is for a particular document. ### Step 5 — Worked Numerical Example Suppose we have a mini-corpus of 3 clinical notes, and we want to compute TF-IDF for the term "**arrhythmia**" in Document 1. | **Document** | **Content (Simplified)** | **Total Words** | **Occurrences of "arrhythmia"** | |---|---|---|---| | $D_1$ | "Patient diagnosed with arrhythmia and hypertension" | 7 | 1 | | $D_2$ | "Arrhythmia detected during routine ECG" | 6 | 1 | | $D_3$ | "Diabetes management plan for elderly patient" | 7 | 0 | **Compute TF for "arrhythmia" in $D_1$:** $$ tf(\text{arrhythmia}, D_1) = \frac{1}{7} \approx 0.1429 $$ **Compute DF for "arrhythmia":** It appears in $D_1$ and $D_2$ → $df = 2$. **Compute IDF for "arrhythmia"** (with $N = 3$): $$ idf(\text{arrhythmia}) = \log \frac{3}{2} = \log(1.5) \approx 0.1761 $$ *(Using natural logarithm; if $\log_{10}$ is used, $idf = 0.1761$ and $tf \cdot idf = 0.0252$.)* **Final TF-IDF weight:** $$ w_{\text{arrhythmia}, D_1} = 0.1429 \times 0.1761 \approx 0.0252 $$ **Clinical Interpretation:** A low absolute value is normal because TF is small. The *relative ranking* matters: "arrhythmia" in $D_1$ is more meaningful than the word "patient" would be, because the IDF penalty for "patient" would be much higher. ## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation: Principal Component Analysis (PCA) for Feature Reduction PCA is the most widely used **linear dimensionality reduction** technique in health informatics. It projects high-dimensional medical feature vectors into a lower-dimensional subspace while preserving maximum variance. ### Step 1 — Center the Data Given a feature matrix $X \in \mathbb{R}^{N \times d}$ (rows = patient samples, columns = features), center each feature column: $$ X_c = X - \bar{X} $$ **Explanation:** $\bar{X}$ is the mean vector of size $1 \times d$. Subtracting it ensures the data is centered at the origin, a prerequisite for PCA. ### Step 2 — Compute the Covariance Matrix $$ \Sigma = \frac{1}{N-1} X_c^{T} X_c $$ **Explanation:** $\Sigma$ is a $d \times d$ symmetric matrix that captures how each pair of features co-varies. Diagonal entries are variances; off-diagonal entries are covariances. ### Step 3 — Eigen-Decomposition $$ \Sigma \mathbf{v}_i = \lambda_i \mathbf{v}_i $$ **Explanation:** $\lambda_i$ is the $i$-th eigenvalue (variance captured by that principal component) and $\mathbf{v}_i$ is the corresponding eigenvector (direction of that component in feature space). ### Step 4 — Sort and Select Top-$k$ Components Sort eigenvalues in descending order $\lambda_1 \geq \lambda_2 \geq \ldots \geq \lambda_d$. Select the top $k$ eigenvectors to form the projection matrix $W_k = [\mathbf{v}_1 \mid \mathbf{v}_2 \mid \ldots \mid \mathbf{v}_k]$. ### Step 5 — Project the Data $$ Z = X_c W_k $$ **Explanation:** $Z \in \mathbb{R}^{N \times k}$ is the reduced feature matrix. Each row of $Z$ is a new $k$-dimensional feature vector that represents the original patient in a compressed but variance-preserving form. ### Step 6 — Explained Variance Ratio The fraction of total information retained is: $$ \text{EVR}_k = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{k} \lambda_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{d} \lambda_i} $$ **Explanation:** A common KTU requirement is to choose $k$ such that $\text{EVR}_k \geq 0.95$, i.e., retain 95% of the original variance. ## 3.3 Comparative Analytical Matrix — Feature Selection vs. Feature Extraction vs. Feature Representation > [!NOTE] > This table is **highly examinable**. Examiners love asking "Differentiate between..." as a 7-mark sub-part. | **Aspect** | **Feature Extraction** | **Feature Selection** | **Feature Representation** | |---|---|---|---| | **Goal** | Create *new* features from raw data | Pick *best subset* of existing features | Encode features in ML-ready form | | **Output Space** | Same or different dimension | Reduced dimension (subset) | Same dimension, transformed scale | | **Techniques** | PCA, LDA, Wavelet, Fourier | Filter, Wrapper, Embedded | Scaling, Encoding, Embedding | | **Information Loss** | Partial (linear combinations) | None (features unchanged) | None (mathematical rescaling) | | **Example (ECG)** | Compute RR interval, QRS width | Select top 5 from 50 features | Normalize RR to [0,1], one-hot encode "rhythm type" | | **Use Case Stage** | Stage 1 of pipeline | Stage 2 of pipeline | Final stage before ML model | # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Complete Feature Engineering Pipeline (Mermaid Flowchart) ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Raw Biomedical Data
ECG, MRI, EHR, Genomic] --> B[Stage 1 Preprocessing
Filtering, Denoising, Imputation] B --> C[Stage 2 Feature Extraction
Time, Frequency, Statistical] C --> D[Stage 3 Feature Selection
Filter, Wrapper, Embedded] D --> E[Stage 4 Feature Representation
Normalization, Encoding, Embedding] E --> F[ML Model
SVM, RF, Neural Net, KNN] F --> G[Clinical Decision
Diagnosis, Prognosis, Risk] ``` **Interpretation of the Flowchart:** - **A → B:** Raw, noisy medical data enters the pipeline. - **B → C:** Cleaned data is transformed into quantitative descriptors. - **C → D:** The most discriminative features are retained; redundant ones discarded. - **D → E:** Surviving features are normalized/encoded to a uniform numerical scale. - **E → F:** The feature vector (now ML-ready) is fed to a learning algorithm. - **F → G:** The model output translates into a clinical decision. ## 4.2 Feature Extraction Methods Taxonomy ```mermaid flowchart LR FE[Feature Extraction Methods] --> TD[Time Domain] FE --> FD[Frequency Domain] FE --> ST[Statistical Morphological] FE --> TF[Time-Frequency Hybrid] FE --> DL[Deep Learning Automated] TD --> T1[Mean, RMS, Variance, ZCR] TD --> T2[Peak Amplitude, RR Interval] FD --> F1[Fourier Transform PSD] FD --> F2[Band Power, Spectral Entropy] FD --> F3[Welch Periodogram] ST --> S1[Skewness, Kurtosis] ST --> S2[GLCM Texture Features] ST --> S3[Histogram Based Bins] TF --> H1[Short Time Fourier Transform] TF --> H2[Wavelet Packet Coefficients] TF --> H3[Hilbert Huang Transform] DL --> D1[CNN Autoencoders] DL --> D2[Transfer Learned Embeddings] DL --> D3[Graph Neural Networks] ``` **Interpretation of Taxonomy:** - **Time-Domain (TD):** Features computed directly from the amplitude-time plot. Cheapest and most interpretable. - **Frequency-Domain (FD):** Features computed in the frequency axis after Fourier transform. Reveals periodic patterns (e.g., heart rate in Hz). - **Statistical/Morphological (ST):** Shape and distribution-based features. Common in radiomics (texture of tumors). - **Time-Frequency (TF):** Hybrid features that capture *how* the frequency content changes over time. Powerful for non-stationary signals like EEG. - **Deep Learning (DL):** Automated feature learning, no manual engineering needed. Used in modern medical imaging. ## 4.3 Feature Representation Strategies (Block Diagram) ```mermaid flowchart TD R[Input Feature Vector] --> R1{Numerical or Categorical} R1 -->|Numerical| N1[Min Max Normalization] R1 -->|Numerical| N2[Z Score Standardization] R1 -->|Numerical| N3[Robust Scaling IQR] R1 -->|Categorical| C1[Label Encoding] R1 -->|Categorical| C2[One Hot Encoding] R1 -->|Categorical| C3[Target Encoding] R1 -->|Text| T1[TF IDF Vectorization] R1 -->|Text| T2[Word2Vec Embedding] R1 -->|Text| T3[BERT Clinical Embedding] N1 --> O[ML Ready Vector] N2 --> O N3 --> O C1 --> O C2 --> O C3 --> O T1 --> O T2 --> O T3 --> O ``` **Interpretation:** The representation strategy is **data-type dependent**: - Numerical clinical measurements (BP, glucose) → scale them. - Categorical clinical attributes (gender, blood group) → encode them as integers or binary vectors. - Free-text clinical notes → vectorize them via TF-IDF or contextual embeddings. The final output **O** is always a fixed-size numerical vector compatible with the downstream ML model. # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Question 1: Define feature extraction and list any four time-domain features used in ECG signal analysis.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Question]` **Model Answer (3 marks):** Feature extraction is the process of deriving a compact set of meaningful numerical descriptors (features) from raw biomedical data, such that the essential diagnostic information is preserved in a form suitable for machine learning algorithms. Four commonly used **time-domain features in ECG** analysis are: 1. **Mean of RR intervals** — average time between successive R-peaks. 2. **Standard deviation of NN intervals (SDNN)** — heart rate variability indicator. 3. **Root Mean Square of Successive Differences (RMSSD)** — short-term HRV. 4. **QRS complex width** — duration of ventricular depolarization. *(1 mark for correct definition, 1 mark for the concept link, 1 mark for listing the four features with brief relevance.)* --- ### **Question 2: What is the difference between feature extraction and feature selection? Give one example of each from medical imaging.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Supplementary]` **Model Answer (3 marks):** | **Basis** | **Feature Extraction** | **Feature Selection** | |---|---|---| | Purpose | Generates *new* features from raw data | Picks *best subset* from existing features | | Nature | Transformation-based | Subset-based | | Example (MRI tumor) | Apply GLCM to compute Haralick texture features (contrast, correlation) | From 200 GLCM features, select top 15 using mutual information criterion | *(1.5 marks for distinction, 1.5 marks for medical-imaging examples.)* --- ## 📕 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > **Note:** As per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, students must answer ONE of the two internal choices provided. Both questions below are fully solved for reference. --- ### **Question A (14 Marks): Comprehensive Feature Engineering for ECG-Based Arrhythmia Detection** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Part B Module 3]` #### **Part (a) — Explain the step-by-step feature extraction process for ECG signals. Discuss time-domain and frequency-domain features in detail. (7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Preprocessing:** The raw ECG signal is band-pass filtered (typically 0.5–40 Hz) using a Butterworth filter to remove baseline wander, powerline interference (50 Hz), and high-frequency noise. **Step 2 — R-peak Detection:** The Pan-Tompkins algorithm identifies R-peaks. RR intervals (time between successive R-peaks) are computed. **Step 3 — Time-Domain Feature Extraction:** From the cleaned signal and RR series: - **Mean RR interval** $\bar{RR} = \frac{1}{N-1} \sum_{i=1}^{N-1} RR_i$ - **SDNN** (Standard Deviation of NN intervals) = $\sqrt{\frac{1}{N-1} \sum (NN_i - \bar{NN})^2}$ - **RMSSD** = $\sqrt{\frac{1}{N-1} \sum_{i=1}^{N-1} (NN_{i+1} - NN_i)^2}$ - **pNN50** = fraction of successive NN differences > 50 ms. **Step 4 — Frequency-Domain Feature Extraction:** Apply Fast Fourier Transform (FFT) to the RR interval series. Compute power in: - **VLF band** (0.003–0.04 Hz) - **LF band** (0.04–0.15 Hz) — sympathetic activity - **HF band** (0.15–0.4 Hz) — parasympathetic activity - **LF/HF ratio** — autonomic balance indicator **Clinical Significance:** Reduced HF power and elevated LF/HF ratio are biomarkers of arrhythmic risk and autonomic dysfunction. *(2 marks for preprocessing and R-peak detection, 2 marks for time-domain features with formulas, 2 marks for frequency-domain features with bands, 1 mark for clinical interpretation.)* --- #### **Part (b) — Describe feature representation techniques (normalization and encoding) used to prepare the extracted features for a classifier. With a numerical example, show how min-max normalization is applied. (7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Why Representation Matters:** Extracted features have different units and scales (e.g., RR interval in ms, QRS width in ms, signal energy in mV²). Without scaling, classifiers like SVM or KNN get biased by high-magnitude features. Normalization resolves this. **Step 2 — Min-Max Normalization Formula:** $$ x'_{i} = \frac{x_i - x_{min}}{x_{max} - x_{min}} $$ **Step 3 — Worked Example:** Consider 5 patients with the following RR intervals (in ms): {600, 720, 850, 580, 910}. - $x_{min} = 580$, $x_{max} = 910$ - Patient 1: $x'_1 = \frac{600 - 580}{910 - 580} = \frac{20}{330} \approx 0.061$ - Patient 2: $x'_2 = \frac{720 - 580}{330} = \frac{140}{330} \approx 0.424$ - Patient 3: $x'_3 = \frac{850 - 580}{330} = \frac{270}{330} \approx 0.818$ - Patient 4: $x'_4 = \frac{580 - 580}{330} = 0.000$ - Patient 5: $x'_5 = \frac{910 - 580}{330} = \frac{330}{330} = 1.000$ **Step 4 — Categorical Encoding:** If we have a feature "Rhythm Type" with values {Normal, AF, VT, PVC}, we apply **one-hot encoding**: | **Patient** | **Normal** | **AF** | **VT** | **PVC** | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | 1 | 0 | 0 | 0 | | 2 | 0 | 1 | 0 | 0 | | 3 | 0 | 0 | 1 | 0 | | 4 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 1 | **Step 5 — Final ML-Ready Vector:** Concatenate scaled numerical features with one-hot encoded categorical features to form the final input vector for the classifier. *(2 marks for justification of representation, 2 marks for min-max formula and step-by-step computation, 2 marks for one-hot encoding, 1 mark for final vector construction.)* --- ### **Question B (14 Marks): Feature Extraction and Representation for Clinical Text Mining** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Part B Module 3]` #### **Part (a) — Explain the TF-IDF feature representation method for clinical text. Derive the formula with all steps. (7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Motivation:** Clinical notes (discharge summaries, radiology reports) contain free-text. ML models require numerical input. TF-IDF converts text into weighted numerical vectors. **Step 2 — Term Frequency (TF):** Normalized frequency of a term in a document: $$ tf(t, d) = \frac{f_{t,d}}{\sum_{t' \in d} f_{t',d}} $$ **Step 3 — Document Frequency (DF):** $$ df(t) = \sum_{d \in D} \mathbf{1}\{t \in d\} $$ **Step 4 — Inverse Document Frequency (IDF):** Reduces weight of common terms: $$ idf(t) = \log \frac{N}{df(t)} $$ **Step 5 — TF-IDF Combination:** $$ w_{t,d} = tf(t, d) \cdot \log \frac{N}{df(t)} $$ **Step 6 — Numerical Example:** Given 3 clinical notes, for the term "**tumor**": - $D_1$ = "tumor detected in left lung" → 6 words, "tumor" appears 1 time → $tf = 1/6 = 0.167$ - $D_2$ = "tumor resection completed" → 4 words, "tumor" appears 1 time → $tf = 0.25$ - $D_3$ = "routine follow-up, no abnormality" → 5 words, "tumor" appears 0 times → $tf = 0$ Assume $N = 3$ and "tumor" appears in 2 documents, so $df = 2$: $$ idf(\text{tumor}) = \log \frac{3}{2} \approx 0.405 $$ Final weight in $D_1$: $w = 0.167 \times 0.405 \approx 0.068$. *(1 mark for motivation, 2 marks for TF and DF formulas, 2 marks for IDF derivation, 2 marks for numerical example with clinical context.)* --- #### **Part (b) — Discuss dimensionality reduction using PCA for high-dimensional biomedical features. Explain the steps and the explained variance ratio. (7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Why Dimensionality Reduction:** Biomedical datasets often have hundreds of features (e.g., radiomics features, gene expression levels). This causes the *curse of dimensionality* — sparsity, overfitting, and high computational cost. PCA reduces the feature count while retaining variance. **Step 2 — Algorithm Steps:** 1. **Center the data:** $X_c = X - \bar{X}$ 2. **Compute covariance matrix:** $\Sigma = \frac{1}{N-1} X_c^{T} X_c$ 3. **Eigen-decomposition:** $\Sigma \mathbf{v}_i = \lambda_i \mathbf{v}_i$ 4. **Sort eigenvalues** $\lambda_1 \geq \lambda_2 \geq \ldots \geq \lambda_d$ and select top $k$ eigenvectors. 5. **Project data:** $Z = X_c W_k$ where $W_k = [\mathbf{v}_1, \ldots, \mathbf{v}_k]$. **Step 3 — Explained Variance Ratio (EVR):** $$ \text{EVR}_k = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{k} \lambda_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{d} \lambda_i} $$ A common selection criterion is $\text{EVR}_k \geq 0.95$, i.e., retain 95% of the original information. **Step 4 — Clinical Example:** In a gene-expression dataset with 20,000 genes per patient, PCA may reduce this to 50 principal components that retain 96% of the variance. This 50-D vector is then used for cancer subtype classification, dramatically reducing computation and overfitting risk. *(1.5 marks for motivation, 2 marks for all 5 algorithm steps, 1.5 marks for EVR formula, 2 marks for clinical example and 95% threshold.)* --- > [!WARNING] > **🚨 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls (Read Carefully!)** > > 1. **Confusing Feature Extraction with Feature Selection** — Examiners deduct up to **2 marks** if you interchange these terms. Remember: *extraction creates new features, selection picks existing ones.* > 2. **Forgetting Units in the Formula Sheet** — Questions like "what is the unit of SNR?" are worth 1 mark. Always state **dB**, **Hz**, **ms**, etc. > 3. **Skipping the Preprocessing Step in ECG Questions** — Even if not asked explicitly, mentioning filtering and R-peak detection adds clinical credibility and fetches **+1 grace mark** from lenient evaluators. > 4. **Mixing Up TF and IDF** — TF is *local* (within a document); IDF is *global* (across the corpus). Writing them backwards costs a full step mark. > 5. **Not Normalizing Before Distance Computation** — If you write "use Euclidean distance on raw features" without first normalizing, mark loss is guaranteed. Always scale first. > 6. **Writing $|x_i|$ inside a markdown table** — Use `\vert x_i \vert` to avoid table-rendering errors in your answer script. --- ## 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) - ✅ **Feature Extraction** = *Create* new features from raw biomedical data (signals, images, text). - ✅ **Feature Selection** = *Choose* best subset from existing features (filter / wrapper / embedded methods). - ✅ **Feature Representation** = *Encode* features into ML-ready numerical form (scaling, one-hot, embeddings). - ✅ **Time-domain features**: mean, std, RMS, ZCR, RR interval, QRS width. - ✅ **Frequency-domain features**: PSD, band power (VLF/LF/HF), spectral entropy. - ✅ **Statistical features**: skewness, kurtosis, GLCM texture descriptors. - ✅ **TF-IDF** is the standard text-to-vector method for clinical NLP; formula is $w_{t,d} = tf(t,d) \cdot \log(N/df_t)$. - ✅ **Min-Max Normalization** rescales features to [0, 1]; formula is $x' = (x - x_{min})/(x_{max} - x_{min})$. - ✅ **Z-score Standardization** centers data with unit variance; formula is $x' = (x - \mu)/\sigma$. - ✅ **PCA** is the go-to linear dimensionality reduction; uses eigen-decomposition of the covariance matrix. - ✅ **EVR (Explained Variance Ratio)** $\geq 0.95$ is the standard threshold for selecting the number of principal components. - ✅ **Distance metrics** (Euclidean, Manhattan, Cosine, Mahalanobis) must be applied on *normalized* features. - ✅ **One-hot encoding** is preferred for nominal categorical features (e.g., blood group); **label encoding** for ordinal features. - ✅ **Always state the unit** (ms, mV, Hz, dB) when discussing biomedical features — this is a free mark booster. - ✅ **Pipeline order** (memorize this exact sequence): Raw Data → Preprocessing → Feature Extraction → Feature Selection → Feature Representation → ML Model → Clinical Decision. - ✅ **KTU Hot Tip**: In 14-mark questions, always include one **clinical interpretation sentence** at the end of each sub-part. Examiners reward clinical relevance.

topics.title: Supervised and Unsupervised Learning Algorithms
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEDAT751 - COMPUTATIONAL HEALTH INFORMATICS
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Supervised and Unsupervised Learning Algorithms

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Supervised Learning — Formal Definition

**Supervised Learning** is a Machine Learning (ML) paradigm in which a computational model is trained on a **labelled dataset** — that is, a dataset where every input sample $\mathbf{x}_i$ is paired with a known target output $y_i$. The model learns an approximation of the unknown mapping function $f: \mathcal{X} \rightarrow \mathcal{Y}$ such that the predicted output $\hat{y} = f(\mathbf{x}; \boldsymbol{\theta})$ generalises well to **unseen data** drawn from the same distribution.

Mathematically, the objective is to minimise an expected risk (generalisation error):

$$\mathcal{R}(\boldsymbol{\theta}) = \mathbb{E}_{(\mathbf{x},y) \sim \mathcal{D}} \left[ \mathcal{L}\big(y, f(\mathbf{x}; \boldsymbol{\theta})\big) \right]$$

where $\mathcal{L}(\cdot)$ is the loss function and $\mathcal{D}$ is the true data distribution. Because the true distribution is unknown, this is approximated by the **empirical risk** over the training set of size $N$:

$$\hat{\mathcal{R}}(\boldsymbol{\theta}) = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \mathcal{L}\big(y_i, f(\mathbf{x}_i; \boldsymbol{\theta})\big)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight — PEDAT751 Module 3**
> Supervised Learning is a **core sub-topic** under "Learning Algorithms for Health Data". Examiners frequently test (a) the difference between classification and regression, and (b) the role of labelled data.

### 1.2 Unsupervised Learning — Formal Definition

**Unsupervised Learning** is the ML paradigm in which the model is trained on an **unlabelled dataset** $\mathcal{X} = \{\mathbf{x}_1, \mathbf{x}_2, \dots, \mathbf{x}_N\}$ where no target outputs are provided. The objective is to discover the **intrinsic structure**, clusters, low-dimensional manifolds, or statistical regularities within the data.

The objective typically takes one of these forms:

- **Clustering:** Maximise intra-cluster similarity $S(\text{intra})$ and inter-cluster separation $S(\text{inter})$.
- **Dimensionality Reduction:** Minimise reconstruction error $\Vert \mathbf{x} - \hat{\mathbf{x}} \Vert^2$ under a low-rank constraint.
- **Density Estimation:** Approximate the true probability density $p(\mathbf{x})$.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **🍎 "The Apple Basket" Analogy**
>
> - **Supervised Learning** = A child is given a basket of apples and a teacher says *"This is a Red Delicious, this is a Granny Smith, this is a Fuji…"* — the child learns from the **labels** and can then classify a new apple.
> - **Unsupervised Learning** = The child is given the same basket but with **no labels**. The child then groups the apples **spontaneously** by colour, size, or shape — discovering structure without guidance.

### 1.4 Sub-Categories of Both Paradigms

| Paradigm | Sub-Category | Output Type | Example Task (Health) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Supervised | **Classification** | Discrete class label $y \in \{1,\dots,K\}$ | Tumour **Malignant vs Benign** |
| Supervised | **Regression** | Continuous value $y \in \mathbb{R}$ | Predicting patient **blood pressure** |
| Unsupervised | **Clustering** | Cluster ID per sample | Grouping patients by **disease subtype** |
| Unsupervised | **Dimensionality Reduction** | Lower-dimensional representation | Visualising **gene expression** in 2D |
| Unsupervised | **Association / Anomaly** | Rules or novelty scores | Detecting **fraudulent insurance claims** |

### 1.5 Standard Metrics & Constants

- **Learning rate**: $\eta \in (0, 1]$ — controls step size in gradient updates.
- **Regularisation strength**: $\lambda \geq 0$ — penalises large weights to prevent **overfitting**.
- **Cluster validity index range**: Silhouette score $\in [-1, +1]$ — **higher is better**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Pipeline of Supervised Learning

The training process follows a **closed-loop cycle**:

1. **Data Acquisition** — collect labelled samples $(\mathbf{x}_i, y_i)$ from the clinical environment.
2. **Pre-processing** — handle missing values (e.g., mean imputation), normalise features (z-score: $z = (x-\mu)/\sigma$), encode categorical variables.
3. **Train / Validation / Test Split** — typical split: **70 % / 15 % / 15 %**.
4. **Model Hypothesis Selection** — pick $f(\mathbf{x}; \boldsymbol{\theta})$ (e.g., linear, tree-based, neural).
5. **Loss Computation** — measure discrepancy between $\hat{y}$ and $y$.
6. **Optimisation** — adjust $\boldsymbol{\theta}$ via **Gradient Descent**: $\boldsymbol{\theta}_{t+1} = \boldsymbol{\theta}_t - \eta \nabla_{\boldsymbol{\theta}}\mathcal{L}$.
7. **Validation & Hyper-parameter Tuning** — cross-validation, grid search.
8. **Generalisation Test** — evaluate on the held-out test set.

### 2.2 Operational Pipeline of Unsupervised Learning

1. **Data Acquisition** — collect unlabelled clinical records.
2. **Feature Engineering** — scaling, encoding, feature selection.
3. **Algorithm Selection** — clustering vs reduction vs density.
4. **Pattern Discovery** — run the algorithm.
5. **Validation** — internal metrics (e.g., Silhouette) or downstream task evaluation.
6. **Interpretation** — domain expert (e.g., clinician) labels discovered clusters.

### 2.3 Key Algorithms in Each Paradigm

#### 2.3.1 Supervised Algorithms (Health Examples)

| Algorithm | Hypothesis | Use in Health |
|---|---|---|
| **Linear Regression (LR)** | $f(\mathbf{x}) = \mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x} + b$ | Predicting **BMI** from diet & activity |
| **Logistic Regression (LogR)** | $p(y=1\mid\mathbf{x}) = \sigma(\mathbf{w}^\top\mathbf{x}+b)$ | **Disease presence** (binary) |
| **Decision Tree (DT)** | Recursive partitioning by Gini / Entropy | Diagnostic **rule extraction** |
| **Random Forest (RF)** | Bagging of DTs | Robust **cancer classification** |
| **Support Vector Machine (SVM)** | Max-margin hyperplane | **Genomic** micro-array classification |
| **K-Nearest Neighbours (KNN)** | Majority vote of $K$ nearest points | Simple **ECG pattern** detection |
| **Naïve Bayes (NB)** | Bayes' theorem + feature independence | **Spam / report** classification |
| **Gradient Boosting (XGBoost)** | Sequential additive ensemble | ICU **mortality prediction** |

#### 2.3.2 Unsupervised Algorithms (Health Examples)

| Algorithm | Mechanism | Use in Health |
|---|---|---|
| **K-Means** | Centroid-based partition | Patient **risk segmentation** |
| **Hierarchical Clustering** | Agglomerative / divisive tree | **Disease taxonomy** discovery |
| **DBSCAN** | Density-based spatial clustering | **Outlier detection** in vitals |
| **PCA** | Eigen-decomposition of covariance | **Gene expression** compression |
| **t-SNE / UMAP** | Non-linear manifold embedding | **Single-cell** RNA-seq visualisation |
| **Autoencoders** | Bottleneck neural network reconstruction | **Medical image** denoising |
| **Apriori / FP-Growth** | Frequent item-set mining | Drug–drug **interaction rules** |

### 2.4 The "Why" Behind Each Paradigm

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Supervised?** — When **ground-truth labels exist** (e.g., radiologist-marked X-rays). The model can directly align predictions to known outcomes and the performance is **measurable** via accuracy, F1, AUC.
>
> **Why Unsupervised?** — When **labels are unavailable, expensive, or impossible** (e.g., novel disease sub-types). The model surfaces hidden patterns and aids **hypothesis generation**.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these equations — they appear almost every exam cycle.**

| # | Concept | Formula | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Linear Regression prediction | $\hat{y} = \mathbf{w}^\top\mathbf{x} + b$ | Vector form |
| 2 | MSE Loss | $\mathcal{L} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}(y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2$ | Mean Squared Error |
| 3 | Normal Equation (closed-form LR) | $\mathbf{w} = (\mathbf{X}^\top\mathbf{X})^{-1}\mathbf{X}^\top\mathbf{y}$ | Requires invertible $\mathbf{X}^\top\mathbf{X}$ |
| 4 | Gradient Descent Update | $\boldsymbol{\theta} \leftarrow \boldsymbol{\theta} - \eta \nabla_{\boldsymbol{\theta}}\mathcal{L}$ | $\eta$ is learning rate |
| 5 | Sigmoid (Logistic) | $\sigma(z) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-z}}$ | $\sigma \in (0,1)$ |
| 6 | Binary Cross-Entropy | $\mathcal{L} = -[y\log\hat{y} + (1-y)\log(1-\hat{y})]$ | Logistic loss |
| 7 | Softmax | $\sigma(\mathbf{z})_i = \frac{e^{z_i}}{\sum_{j} e^{z_j}}$ | Multi-class |
| 8 | Gini Impurity (DT) | $G = 1 - \sum_{k=1}^{K} p_k^2$ | $\in [0, 1-1/K]$ |
| 9 | Entropy | $H = -\sum_{k=1}^{K} p_k \log_2 p_k$ | Higher = more disorder |
| 10 | K-Means Objective (SSE) | $J = \sum_{k=1}^{K}\sum_{\mathbf{x}_i \in C_k} \Vert \mathbf{x}_i - \boldsymbol{\mu}_k \Vert^2$ | Minimised iteratively |
| 11 | Silhouette Score | $s(i) = \frac{b(i)-a(i)}{\max\{a(i), b(i)\}}$ | $\in [-1,+1]$ |
| 12 | PCA Reconstruction | $\hat{\mathbf{x}} = \mathbf{W}_d \mathbf{W}_d^\top (\mathbf{x} - \boldsymbol{\mu}) + \boldsymbol{\mu}$ | $\mathbf{W}_d$ = top-$d$ eigenvectors |
| 13 | L2 Regularisation | $\mathcal{L}_{\text{reg}} = \mathcal{L} + \lambda \Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert_2^2$ | Weight decay |
| 14 | Bayes' Theorem | $P(C_k \mid \mathbf{x}) = \frac{P(\mathbf{x}\mid C_k)P(C_k)}{P(\mathbf{x})}$ | NB foundation |
| 15 | SVM Decision Function | $f(\mathbf{x}) = \text{sign}\big(\mathbf{w}^\top\mathbf{x} + b\big)$ | Linear separable case |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Use `\vert` or `\mid` instead of `|` inside table rows** (e.g., $\boldsymbol{\theta} \vert \nabla_{\boldsymbol{\theta}}\mathcal{L}\vert$ is invalid in a table — prefer $\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert$). This avoids breaking markdown table syntax in KTU's automated rendering pipeline.

### 2.6 Real-World Utility in Health Informatics

- **Supervised** models power **Clinical Decision Support Systems (CDSS)** by predicting readmission, sepsis, or mortality using Electronic Health Records (EHR).
- **Unsupervised** models enable **patient stratification** for personalised treatment — e.g., discovering Type-2 Diabetes sub-phenotypes in EHR data (a well-known 2018 *Lancet Digital Health* result).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Linear Regression via the Normal Equation

We derive the closed-form solution for $\mathbf{w}$ that minimises the MSE loss.

**Step 1 — Express the dataset in matrix form.**

Let $\mathbf{X} \in \mathbb{R}^{N \times (d+1)}$ be the design matrix with a leading column of 1s (for the bias $b$):

$$\mathbf{X} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & x_{1,1} & x_{1,2} & \cdots & x_{1,d} \\ 1 & x_{2,1} & x_{2,2} & \cdots & x_{2,d} \\ \vdots & \vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots \\ 1 & x_{N,1} & x_{N,2} & \cdots & x_{N,d} \end{bmatrix}, \quad \mathbf{y} \in \mathbb{R}^{N}$$

The parameter vector (including bias) is $\boldsymbol{\theta} = [b, w_1, w_2, \dots, w_d]^\top \in \mathbb{R}^{d+1}$.

**Step 2 — Write the prediction vector.**

$$\hat{\mathbf{y}} = \mathbf{X}\boldsymbol{\theta}$$

**Step 3 — Express the MSE as a quadratic function of $\boldsymbol{\theta}$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathcal{L}(\boldsymbol{\theta}) &= \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} (y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2 \\
&= \frac{1}{N} (\mathbf{y} - \mathbf{X}\boldsymbol{\theta})^\top (\mathbf{y} - \mathbf{X}\boldsymbol{\theta}) \\
&= \frac{1}{N}\left(\mathbf{y}^\top\mathbf{y} - 2\boldsymbol{\theta}^\top\mathbf{X}^\top\mathbf{y} + \boldsymbol{\theta}^\top\mathbf{X}^\top\mathbf{X}\boldsymbol{\theta}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the gradient with respect to $\boldsymbol{\theta}$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\nabla_{\boldsymbol{\theta}} \mathcal{L}(\boldsymbol{\theta}) &= \frac{1}{N}\left(-2\mathbf{X}^\top\mathbf{y} + 2\mathbf{X}^\top\mathbf{X}\boldsymbol{\theta}\right) \\
&= \frac{2}{N}\left(\mathbf{X}^\top\mathbf{X}\boldsymbol{\theta} - \mathbf{X}^\top\mathbf{y}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Set the gradient to zero to find the optimum.**

$$\mathbf{X}^\top\mathbf{X}\boldsymbol{\theta} = \mathbf{X}^\top\mathbf{y}$$

**Step 6 — Solve the linear system by multiplying both sides by $(\mathbf{X}^\top\mathbf{X})^{-1}$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\boldsymbol{\theta}^{*} &= (\mathbf{X}^\top\mathbf{X})^{-1}\mathbf{X}^\top\mathbf{y} \\
\boxed{\mathbf{w} &= (\mathbf{X}^\top\mathbf{X})^{-1}\mathbf{X}^\top\mathbf{y}}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the **Normal Equation**. The matrix $\mathbf{X}^\dagger = (\mathbf{X}^\top\mathbf{X})^{-1}\mathbf{X}^\top$ is the **Moore–Penrose pseudo-inverse** for full-rank square $\mathbf{X}^\top\mathbf{X}$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall** — The Normal Equation requires $\mathbf{X}^\top\mathbf{X}$ to be **invertible**. If features are correlated (multicollinearity) — common in clinical data — the matrix becomes ill-conditioned. Use **ridge regression** instead: add $\lambda\mathbf{I}$ to the diagonal.

### 3.2 Derivation: Gradient Descent for Logistic Regression

The hypothesis is the sigmoid: $h_{\boldsymbol{\theta}}(\mathbf{x}) = \sigma(\boldsymbol{\theta}^\top\mathbf{x}) = \frac{1}{1+e^{-\boldsymbol{\theta}^\top\mathbf{x}}}$.

**Step 1 — Define the loss (Binary Cross-Entropy) for a single sample.**

$$
\mathcal{L}^{(i)} = -\big[y_i \log h_{\boldsymbol{\theta}}(\mathbf{x}_i) + (1-y_i)\log(1 - h_{\boldsymbol{\theta}}(\mathbf{x}_i))\big]
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the gradient using the chain rule.**

Using the well-known identity $\frac{d\sigma(z)}{dz} = \sigma(z)(1-\sigma(z))$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}^{(i)}}{\partial \boldsymbol{\theta}} &= \left( \frac{y_i}{\sigma(\boldsymbol{\theta}^\top\mathbf{x}_i)} - \frac{1-y_i}{1-\sigma(\boldsymbol{\theta}^\top\mathbf{x}_i)} \right) \cdot \frac{\partial \sigma}{\partial \boldsymbol{\theta}} \\
&= \left( \frac{y_i - \sigma(\boldsymbol{\theta}^\top\mathbf{x}_i)}{\sigma(\boldsymbol{\theta}^\top\mathbf{x}_i)\big(1-\sigma(\boldsymbol{\theta}^\top\mathbf{x}_i)\big)} \right) \cdot \sigma(\boldsymbol{\theta}^\top\mathbf{x}_i)\big(1-\sigma(\boldsymbol{\theta}^\top\mathbf{x}_i)\big) \mathbf{x}_i \\
&= \big(y_i - h_{\boldsymbol{\theta}}(\mathbf{x}_i)\big)\mathbf{x}_i
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Gradient Descent update rule.**

$$\boldsymbol{\theta} \leftarrow \boldsymbol{\theta} + \eta \big(y_i - h_{\boldsymbol{\theta}}(\mathbf{x}_i)\big)\mathbf{x}_i$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation** — The update is the **prediction error** $(y_i - \hat{y}_i)$ multiplied by the input $\mathbf{x}_i$ and learning rate $\eta$. The same form as Adaline (Widrow-Hoff) rule — elegant and computationally cheap.

### 3.3 Derivation: K-Means Algorithm Update

**Step 1 — Initialisation.** Pick $K$ initial centroids $\{\boldsymbol{\mu}_1, \dots, \boldsymbol{\mu}_K\}$ (e.g., by random sampling or K-Means++).

**Step 2 — Assignment step.** For each point $\mathbf{x}_i$, assign to the nearest centroid:

$$C_k = \{\mathbf{x}_i : k = \arg\min_{j} \Vert \mathbf{x}_i - \boldsymbol{\mu}_j \Vert^2\}$$

**Step 3 — Update step.** Recompute each centroid as the mean of all points assigned to it:

$$\boldsymbol{\mu}_k = \frac{1}{\vert C_k \vert}\sum_{\mathbf{x}_i \in C_k}\mathbf{x}_i$$

**Step 4 — Convergence.** Repeat steps 2–3 until the cluster assignments no longer change or the change in $J = \sum_{k=1}^{K}\sum_{\mathbf{x}_i \in C_k}\Vert \mathbf{x}_i - \boldsymbol{\mu}_k \Vert^2$ falls below a tolerance $\varepsilon$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Convergence Proof Sketch (KTU favourite):** Each assignment step minimises $J$ w.r.t. the assignments $\{C_k\}$ for fixed centroids. Each update step minimises $J$ w.r.t. the centroids $\{\boldsymbol{\mu}_k\}$ for fixed assignments. Since $J$ is bounded below by $0$ and strictly decreases, the algorithm **converges in finite steps**.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Supervised vs. Unsupervised

| Dimension | Supervised | Unsupervised |
|---|---|---|
| **Data** | Labelled $(\mathbf{x}, y)$ | Unlabelled $\mathbf{x}$ |
| **Goal** | Learn $f: \mathcal{X} \rightarrow \mathcal{Y}$ | Discover structure $p(\mathbf{x})$ |
| **Loss** | Explicit ($\mathcal{L}(y,\hat{y})$) | Implicit (SSE, reconstruction, density) |
| **Evaluation** | Accuracy, F1, AUC | Silhouette, Davies–Bouldin, visual |
| **Computational Cost** | Higher (labelling expensive) | Lower (no labels) |
| **Output Determinism** | Predictable, reproducible | Sensitive to initialisation |
| **Health Use** | Diagnosis, prognosis | Sub-typing, anomaly detection |
| **Risk** | Overfitting to noisy labels | Discovering spurious clusters |
| **Interpretability** | High (linear models, trees) | Moderate (depends on algorithm) |

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Algorithm Selection for Common Health Tasks

| Task | Data Type | Recommended Algorithm | Rationale |
|---|---|---|---|
| Predict ICU mortality | Tabular EHR | XGBoost / Logistic Regression | Strong on tabular, interpretable |
| Classify skin lesions | Images | CNN (Supervised) | State-of-the-art for vision |
| Group patients by phenotype | Tabular EHR | K-Means / Hierarchical | Unlabelled sub-typing |
| Detect fraudulent claims | Transactional | Isolation Forest / DBSCAN | Outlier detection |
| Visualise gene expression | High-dim genomic | t-SNE / UMAP | Manifold learning |
| Predict patient LOS | Time-series | LSTM (Supervised) | Sequential modelling |
| Cluster similar ECG beats | Time-series | K-Means + DTW | Shape-based grouping |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 High-Level Taxonomy of Learning Paradigms

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Machine Learning] --> B[Supervised Learning]
    A --> C[Unsupervised Learning]
    A --> D[Reinforcement Learning]
    B --> B1[Classification]
    B --> B2[Regression]
    B1 --> B1a[Logistic Regression]
    B1 --> B1b[Decision Tree]
    B1 --> B1c[SVM]
    B1 --> B1d[KNN]
    B1 --> B1e[Naive Bayes]
    B2 --> B2a[Linear Regression]
    B2 --> B2b[Polynomial Regression]
    B2 --> B2c[Random Forest Regressor]
    C --> C1[Clustering]
    C --> C2[Dimensionality Reduction]
    C --> C3[Anomaly Detection]
    C1 --> C1a[K Means]
    C1 --> C1b[Hierarchical]
    C1 --> C1c[DBSCAN]
    C2 --> C2a[PCA]
    C2 --> C2b[t SNE]
    C2 --> C2c[UMAP]
    C3 --> C3a[Isolation Forest]
    C3 --> C3b[One Class SVM]
```

### 4.2 Supervised Learning Pipeline (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Labelled EHR Data] --> A2[Preprocessing]
    A2 --> A3[Train Val Test Split]
    A3 --> A4[Model Training]
    A4 --> A5[Hyperparameter Tuning]
    A5 --> A6{Performance OK}
    A6 -- Yes --> A7[Deploy to Clinical Workflow]
    A6 -- No --> A4
    A7 --> A8[Predict on New Patient]
    A8 --> A9[Clinician Review]
    A9 --> A10[Outcome Feedback]
    A10 -.-> A1
```

### 4.3 Unsupervised Learning Pipeline (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    B1[Unlabelled Clinical Data] --> B2[Preprocessing and Scaling]
    B2 --> B3[Algorithm Selection]
    B3 --> B4[Pattern Discovery]
    B4 --> B5[Internal Validation]
    B5 --> B6{Quality Acceptable}
    B6 -- Yes --> B7[Clinical Interpretation]
    B6 -- No --> B3
    B7 --> B8[New Hypothesis Generated]
    B8 --> B9[Optional Labelling for Supervised Use]
```

### 4.4 Decision Flow — When to Use Which Paradigm

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1{Are labels available?}
    Q1 -- Yes --> Q2{Discrete or continuous output?}
    Q1 -- No --> Q3{Goal type?}
    Q2 -- Discrete --> SUP1[Use Classification]
    Q2 -- Continuous --> SUP2[Use Regression]
    Q3 -- Grouping --> UNS1[Use Clustering]
    Q3 -- Compression --> UNS2[Use Dimensionality Reduction]
    Q3 -- Anomalies --> UNS3[Use Anomaly Detection]
    SUP1 --> ALG1[LR SVM DT RF NB KNN]
    SUP2 --> ALG2[LR PR RFR]
    UNS1 --> ALG3[KMeans Hierarchical DBSCAN]
    UNS2 --> ALG4[PCA tSNE UMAP Autoencoders]
    UNS3 --> ALG5[IsolationForest OneClassSVM]
```

### 4.5 K-Means Iterative Process (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    S1[Start: Choose K] --> S2[Initialise K Centroids]
    S2 --> S3[Assign each point to nearest centroid]
    S3 --> S4[Recompute centroids as cluster mean]
    S4 --> S5{Assignments changed?}
    S5 -- Yes --> S3
    S5 -- No --> S6[Converged Output Final Clusters]
```

### 4.6 Supervised vs. Unsupervised — Side-by-Side Data Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SUP[Supervised]
        X1[Input Features] --> H1[Model f]
        Y1[True Labels] --> L1[Loss Function]

        P1 --> L1
        L1 --> O1[Trained Model]
    end
    subgraph UNS[Unsupervised]

        H2 --> O2[Structure: Clusters Embeddings]
    end
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
> **Differentiate between Supervised and Unsupervised Learning. Give one healthcare example of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Supervised | Unsupervised |
|---|---|---|
| **Labels** | Uses labelled data $(x, y)$ | Uses unlabelled data $x$ |
| **Goal** | Learn mapping $f: X \to Y$ | Discover hidden structure in $X$ |
| **Feedback** | Direct error signal available | No error signal — uses internal criteria |
| **Healthcare example** | Predicting **diabetes (yes/no)** from patient vitals using Logistic Regression | Clustering **patients** by disease severity using K-Means |

*(1 mark for each correct difference point + 1 mark for the healthcare examples.)*

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
> **Explain the working of the K-Means clustering algorithm. Mention its objective function.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

K-Means partitions $N$ data points into $K$ disjoint clusters by minimising the **Sum of Squared Errors (SSE)** objective:

$$J = \sum_{k=1}^{K} \sum_{\mathbf{x}_i \in C_k} \Vert \mathbf{x}_i - \boldsymbol{\mu}_k \Vert^2$$

**Working:**
1. **Initialisation:** Choose $K$ initial centroids (e.g., via K-Means++).
2. **Assignment step:** Assign each point to the cluster with the **nearest centroid** (Euclidean distance).
3. **Update step:** Recompute each centroid as the **mean** of points in its cluster.
4. **Repeat** steps 2–3 until cluster assignments stabilise.

*(1 mark for objective function, 1 mark for the iterative steps, 1 mark for convergence condition.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> ### **Question A — 14 Marks [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Style]**

#### **(a) [7 marks] Derive the Normal Equation for Linear Regression. Under what condition is it preferred over Gradient Descent?**

**Model Solution:**

**Derivation:**

Let $\mathbf{X} \in \mathbb{R}^{N \times (d+1)}$ be the design matrix (with a bias column of 1s) and $\mathbf{y} \in \mathbb{R}^{N}$ the target vector. Hypothesis: $\hat{\mathbf{y}} = \mathbf{X}\boldsymbol{\theta}$.

**Step 1 — Write the MSE loss:**

$$
\mathcal{L}(\boldsymbol{\theta}) = \frac{1}{N} \Vert \mathbf{y} - \mathbf{X}\boldsymbol{\theta} \Vert_2^2 = \frac{1}{N} (\mathbf{y} - \mathbf{X}\boldsymbol{\theta})^\top (\mathbf{y} - \mathbf{X}\boldsymbol{\theta})
$$

**Step 2 — Expand:**

$$
\mathcal{L}(\boldsymbol{\theta}) = \frac{1}{N} \big( \mathbf{y}^\top \mathbf{y} - 2\boldsymbol{\theta}^\top \mathbf{X}^\top \mathbf{y} + \boldsymbol{\theta}^\top \mathbf{X}^\top \mathbf{X} \boldsymbol{\theta} \big)
$$

**Step 3 — Differentiate w.r.t. $\boldsymbol{\theta}$:**

$$
\nabla_{\boldsymbol{\theta}} \mathcal{L} = \frac{2}{N} \big( \mathbf{X}^\top \mathbf{X} \boldsymbol{\theta} - \mathbf{X}^\top \mathbf{y} \big)
$$

**Step 4 — Set gradient to zero:**

$$
\mathbf{X}^\top \mathbf{X} \boldsymbol{\theta} = \mathbf{X}^\top \mathbf{y}
$$

**Step 5 — Solve:**

$$
\boxed{\boldsymbol{\theta}^* = (\mathbf{X}^\top \mathbf{X})^{-1} \mathbf{X}^\top \mathbf{y}}
$$

**Conditions for Preference over Gradient Descent (1 mark):**
- Dataset is **small-to-moderate** ($\mathbf{X}^\top \mathbf{X}$ can be inverted efficiently).
- $\mathbf{X}^\top \mathbf{X}$ is **invertible** (no perfect multicollinearity).
- A **closed-form** solution is acceptable (no need for iterative tuning of $\eta$).

*(Mark split: 5 marks derivation + 1 mark condition + 1 mark conclusion/discussion.)*

---

#### **(b) [7 marks] Explain Logistic Regression as a classification algorithm. Derive the gradient of the Binary Cross-Entropy loss with respect to the weight vector $\boldsymbol{\theta}$.**

**Model Solution:**

**Concept (2 marks):** Logistic Regression models the probability that a sample $\mathbf{x}$ belongs to class 1 using the sigmoid function:

$$h_{\boldsymbol{\theta}}(\mathbf{x}) = \sigma(\boldsymbol{\theta}^\top \mathbf{x}) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-\boldsymbol{\theta}^\top \mathbf{x}}}$$

**Binary Cross-Entropy loss for a single sample:**

$$
\mathcal{L}^{(i)} = - \big[ y_i \log h_{\boldsymbol{\theta}}(\mathbf{x}_i) + (1 - y_i) \log(1 - h_{\boldsymbol{\theta}}(\mathbf{x}_i)) \big]
$$

**Derivation of the gradient (4 marks):**

Using $\sigma'(z) = \sigma(z)(1-\sigma(z))$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}^{(i)}}{\partial \boldsymbol{\theta}} &= -\left[ \frac{y_i}{h} - \frac{1 - y_i}{1 - h} \right] \cdot \frac{\partial h}{\partial \boldsymbol{\theta}} \\
&= -\left[ \frac{y_i (1 - h) - (1 - y_i) h}{h(1 - h)} \right] \cdot h(1 - h)\,\mathbf{x}_i \\
&= -\big[ y_i - h_{\boldsymbol{\theta}}(\mathbf{x}_i) \big] \mathbf{x}_i
\end{aligned}
$$

**Update rule:**

$$
\boldsymbol{\theta} \leftarrow \boldsymbol{\theta} + \eta \big[ y_i - h_{\boldsymbol{\theta}}(\mathbf{x}_i) \big] \mathbf{x}_i
$$

**Conclusion (1 mark):** The update direction is the prediction error $(y_i - \hat{y}_i)$ weighted by the input — analogous to the perceptron / Adaline rule, which gives Logistic Regression a clean probabilistic interpretation.

---

> ### **Question B — 14 Marks [Alternative Choice]**

#### **(a) [7 marks] Describe the K-Means clustering algorithm in detail. Show that each iteration monotonically decreases the objective function $J$.**

**Model Solution:**

**Algorithm (3 marks):**
1. Initialise $K$ centroids $\{\boldsymbol{\mu}_1, \dots, \boldsymbol{\mu}_K\}$.
2. **Assignment step:** $C_k = \{\mathbf{x}_i : k = \arg\min_j \Vert \mathbf{x}_i - \boldsymbol{\mu}_j \Vert^2 \}$.
3. **Update step:** $\boldsymbol{\mu}_k = \frac{1}{\vert C_k \vert} \sum_{\mathbf{x}_i \in C_k} \mathbf{x}_i$.
4. Iterate until convergence.

**Monotonic Decrease Proof (4 marks):**

The objective is:

$$J(\mathcal{C}, \mathcal{M}) = \sum_{k=1}^{K} \sum_{\mathbf{x}_i \in C_k} \Vert \mathbf{x}_i - \boldsymbol{\mu}_k \Vert^2$$

- **Step 2 (Assignment) — for fixed $\mathcal{M}$:** Reassigning $\mathbf{x}_i$ to the cluster with the *nearest* centroid minimises $\Vert \mathbf{x}_i - \boldsymbol{\mu}_k \Vert^2$ for each $i$. Hence $J_{\text{new}} \leq J_{\text{old}}$.
- **Step 3 (Update) — for fixed $\mathcal{C}$:** The centroid that minimises $\sum_{\mathbf{x}_i \in C_k} \Vert \mathbf{x}_i - \boldsymbol{\mu}_k \Vert^2$ is the **mean** of $C_k$ (follows from setting $\partial J / \partial \boldsymbol{\mu}_k = 0$). Hence $J$ again does not increase.

**Conclusion:** $J$ is bounded below by $0$ and monotonically non-increasing ⇒ **converges in finite iterations**.

---

#### **(b) [7 marks] Explain Principal Component Analysis (PCA) as a dimensionality reduction technique. Derive the optimisation objective and its solution.**

**Model Solution:**

**Intuition (2 marks):** PCA finds an orthogonal linear projection that **maximises the variance** of the data in the projected space — equivalently, minimises the **reconstruction error**.

**Setup:** Centre the data $\mathbf{x}_i' = \mathbf{x}_i - \boldsymbol{\mu}$. The sample covariance is:

$$\mathbf{S} = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} (\mathbf{x}_i - \boldsymbol{\mu})(\mathbf{x}_i - \boldsymbol{\mu})^\top$$

**Objective — Maximise projected variance (3 marks):**

For a unit direction $\mathbf{w}$ ($\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert = 1$):

$$
\max_{\mathbf{w}} \; \mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{S} \mathbf{w} \quad \text{subject to} \quad \mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{w} = 1
$$

**Solution via Lagrange multipliers (2 marks):**

$$
\mathcal{L}(\mathbf{w}, \lambda) = \mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{S} \mathbf{w} - \lambda(\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{w} - 1)
$$

Differentiate and set to zero:

$$
\nabla_{\mathbf{w}} \mathcal{L} = 2\mathbf{S}\mathbf{w} - 2\lambda \mathbf{w} = 0 \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; \boxed{\mathbf{S}\mathbf{w} = \lambda \mathbf{w}}
$$

Hence the optimal $\mathbf{w}$ is the **eigenvector** of $\mathbf{S}$ corresponding to the **largest eigenvalue** $\lambda_1$. The top-$d$ principal components are the top-$d$ eigenvectors of $\mathbf{S}$.

**Conclusion:** The variance retained is $\sum_{i=1}^{d} \lambda_i$ (where $\lambda_1 \geq \lambda_2 \geq \dots$).

---

### 5.3 Examiner's Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **🚨 KTU 2024 Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks**
>
> 1. **Confusing "Unsupervised" with "Self-supervised"** — Unsupervised learning uses **no labels**; self-supervised creates pseudo-labels (e.g., masked tokens). Examiners deduct **1 mark** if conflated.
> 2. **Forgetting the bias column in LR** — When deriving the Normal Equation, $\mathbf{X}$ must include a column of 1s. Omitting this is a **2-mark deduction**.
> 3. **Saying "K-Means always finds the global optimum"** — WRONG. K-Means is **NP-hard** in general; it converges to a *local* minimum. The initialisation matters — use K-Means++. This is a frequent trick question.
> 4. **Mixing up classification and regression metrics** — Accuracy/F1 for classification; MSE/MAE/$R^2$ for regression. **1 mark deduction** for confusion.
> 5. **Writing $|x|$ in markdown tables** — Breaks the table renderer. Use $\Vert x \Vert$ instead. KTU's auto-grader may not parse broken tables.
> 6. **Skipping the convergence proof for K-Means** — At least state "bounded below by 0" and "monotonically non-increasing" to get full marks.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **📋 Rapid Revision Checklist (PEDAT751 — Module 3)**
>
> ✅ **Supervised Learning** requires **labelled data** $(x, y)$; goal is to learn $f: X \to Y$.
> ✅ **Unsupervised Learning** uses **unlabelled data**; goal is to discover structure.
> ✅ **Classification** → discrete output (Logistic Reg, SVM, DT, RF, KNN, NB).
> ✅ **Regression** → continuous output (Linear Reg, Polynomial Reg, SVR).
> ✅ **Clustering** → K-Means, Hierarchical, DBSCAN.
> ✅ **Dimensionality Reduction** → PCA, t-SNE, UMAP, Autoencoders.
> ✅ **Normal Equation:** $\boldsymbol{\theta} = (\mathbf{X}^\top\mathbf{X})^{-1}\mathbf{X}^\top\mathbf{y}$.
> ✅ **Gradient Descent:** $\boldsymbol{\theta} \leftarrow \boldsymbol{\theta} - \eta \nabla_{\boldsymbol{\theta}}\mathcal{L}$.
> ✅ **Sigmoid:** $\sigma(z) = 1/(1+e^{-z})$; **Softmax** for multi-class.
> ✅ **K-Means Objective:** $J = \sum_k \sum_{i \in C_k} \Vert \mathbf{x}_i - \boldsymbol{\mu}_k \Vert^2$.
> ✅ **PCA:** eigenvectors of covariance matrix = principal components.
> ✅ **Gini:** $G = 1 - \sum p_k^2$; **Entropy:** $H = -\sum p_k \log_2 p_k$.
> ✅ **Silhouette score** $\in [-1, +1]$ — best unsupervised cluster metric.
> ✅ **Bias–Variance Tradeoff:** regularisation $\lambda$ controls model complexity.
> ✅ **K-Means converges to LOCAL optimum, not global** — use K-Means++.
> ✅ **Health Examples to remember:** Diabetes prediction (Supervised), Patient sub-typing (Unsupervised), ECG anomaly detection (both paradigms).
> ✅ Always **centre and scale** features before PCA / K-Means.
> ✅ **Overfitting prevention:** cross-validation, regularisation, more data.
> ✅ **Evaluation:** For classification use F1/AUC; for regression use RMSE/$R^2$; for clustering use Silhouette.
> ✅ **Key exam keywords to use verbatim:** "empirical risk", "generalisation", "labelled data", "intrinsic structure", "monotonic convergence", "eigen-decomposition".

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Classification and Regression Techniques for Medical Image Analysis
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEDAT751 - COMPUTATIONAL HEALTH INFORMATICS
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Classification and Regression Techniques for Medical Image Analysis

# Classification and Regression Techniques for Medical Image Analysis

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Classification** in medical image analysis is a **supervised learning paradigm** where a model learns a mapping $f: \mathcal{X} \rightarrow \mathcal{Y}$ from a feature space $\mathcal{X} \subseteq \mathbb{R}^{d}$ to a discrete categorical label set $\mathcal{Y} = \{1, 2, \dots, K\}$ (e.g., *malignant / benign*, *tumor / non-tumor*, *NORMAL / PNEUMONIA* on chest X-rays).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Regression** is a **supervised learning paradigm** where the model learns a mapping $f: \mathcal{X} \rightarrow \mathcal{Y}$ from features $\mathcal{X}$ to a *continuous* target variable $y \in \mathbb{R}$ (e.g., tumor volume in mm³, estimated glomerular filtration rate (eGFR), patient age from a retinal fundus image, or bone age from a hand X-ray).

Both techniques form the **analytical core of Computer-Aided Detection (CADe)** and **Computer-Aided Diagnosis (CADx)** systems mandated under modern Clinical Decision Support Systems (CDSS).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Radiologist's Apprentice:**
> Imagine a young radiology resident learning to interpret MRI scans. Over thousands of supervised cases, the resident learns *patterns* — irregular borders + heterogeneous texture → likely **malignant** (Classification: discrete label). Simultaneously, the resident learns to estimate the **diameter** of a tumor by visually scanning it → that is **Regression** (continuous value).
>
> Classification answers **"WHAT is it?"** (malignant vs benign).
> Regression answers **"HOW MUCH / HOW BIG?"** (tumor diameter in cm).

### 1.3 Distinguishing Boundary

| Property | Classification | Regression |
|---|---|---|
| Output Type | Discrete / Categorical | Continuous / Numerical |
| Question Answered | "Which class?" | "How much?" |
| Evaluation Metric Class | Accuracy, F1, AUC-ROC | MSE, MAE, $R^2$ |
| Loss Function | Cross-Entropy, Hinge | Squared Error, Huber |
| Example in Medical Imaging | COVID-19 detection on CT | Brain tumor volume estimation |

### 1.4 Pipeline Position in Medical Image Analytics

```
Raw DICOM Image  →  Preprocessing (denoise, normalize, augment)
                →  Feature Extraction (radiomics / deep features)
                →  ★ CLASSIFIER or REGRESSOR ★
                →  Clinical Decision / Report
```

The classifier/regressor is the **decision-making kernel** of the entire pipeline. Everything before it provides the *evidence*, everything after it consumes the *verdict*.

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification Techniques — Theoretical Breakdown

### 2.1.1 Logistic Regression (Baseline Linear Classifier)

Despite its name, logistic regression is a **classification algorithm**.

**Operational Logic (step-by-step):**
1. Compute a linear combination of input features: $z = \mathbf{w}^{\top}\mathbf{x} + b$.
2. Squash the linear score through the **sigmoid activation** $\sigma(z) = \dfrac{1}{1+e^{-z}}$, mapping $z \in \mathbb{R}$ to a probability $p \in (0,1)$.
3. Threshold at $0.5$: predict class 1 if $p \geq 0.5$, else class 0.

**Why it works for medical imaging:** Extremely fast inference (suitable for point-of-care devices), highly interpretable (odds ratios map to clinical risk factors), and serves as the **clinical baseline** against which deep models must be benchmarked.

### 2.1.2 Support Vector Machines (SVM)

**Operational Logic:**
1. Find the **maximum-margin hyperplane** separating two classes in feature space.
2. Maximize the geometric margin $\gamma = \dfrac{2}{\lVert \mathbf{w} \rVert}$.
3. Use **kernel trick** $\kappa(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j) = \phi(\mathbf{x}_i)^{\top}\phi(\mathbf{x}_j)$ for non-linear boundaries (RBF kernel is standard for radiomics).
4. Soft-margin slack variables $\xi_i$ tolerate misclassifications in noisy clinical data.

**Why it works for medical imaging:** Excels in **high-dimensional, low-sample-size (HDLSS)** regimes — typical in genomics and radiomics where $d \gg n$. Common kernels: **linear**, **polynomial** (degree $p$), and **Radial Basis Function (RBF)** $\kappa(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j) = \exp(-\gamma \lVert \mathbf{x}_i - \mathbf{x}_j \rVert^2)$.

### 2.1.3 k-Nearest Neighbors (k-NN)

**Operational Logic:**
1. Store all training feature vectors $\mathcal{D}_{\text{train}} = \{(\mathbf{x}_i, y_i)\}_{i=1}^{n}$.
2. For a query $\mathbf{x}_q$, compute distance to every training point (Euclidean, Manhattan, Mahalanobis).
3. Vote among the **k closest** training samples.
4. For regression: average the $k$ target values; for classification: majority vote.

**Why it works for medical imaging:** Non-parametric, no training phase; useful as a **sanity-check baseline**. **Caveat:** distance metrics in high-dimensional radiomic spaces suffer from the **curse of dimensionality**.

### 2.1.4 Decision Trees and Random Forests

- A **Decision Tree** recursively partitions the feature space using axis-aligned splits that maximize **Information Gain** $\text{IG}(S, A) = H(S) - \sum_{v \in \text{Values}(A)} \dfrac{\lvert S_v \rvert}{\lvert S \rvert} H(S_v)$, where $H(S) = -\sum_{c} p_c \log_2 p_c$ is Shannon entropy.
- A **Random Forest** is an ensemble of $B$ decorrelated trees trained on bootstrap samples with random feature subsets at each split — reduces variance dramatically.

**Why it works for medical imaging:** Provides **inherent feature importance rankings**, prized by clinicians for explainability (regulatory requirement under EU MDR and FDA SaMD frameworks).

### 2.1.5 Convolutional Neural Networks (CNN) — Deep Classifier

CNN stacks **convolutional**, **activation** (ReLU), **pooling**, and **fully-connected** layers to learn hierarchical features from raw pixels — eliminating the manual radiomics pipeline. Architectures: **ResNet-50**, **DenseNet-121**, **EfficientNet-B0**, **U-Net** (segmentation). Transfer learning from ImageNet weights is the clinical default for small medical datasets.

### 2.1.6 k-Means Clustering (Unsupervised Reference)

Although the question covers *supervised* classification, **k-Means** is the unsupervised reference baseline — it groups pixels/voxels into $k$ clusters by minimizing intra-cluster variance $J = \sum_{i=1}^{k} \sum_{\mathbf{x} \in C_i} \lVert \mathbf{x} - \boldsymbol{\mu}_i \rVert^2$.

## 2.2 Regression Techniques — Theoretical Breakdown

### 2.2.1 Linear Regression

Models the target as a linear combination: $\hat{y} = \mathbf{w}^{\top}\mathbf{x} + b$. The closed-form **Normal Equation** gives the optimum:
$$\mathbf{w}^{*} = (X^{\top}X)^{-1} X^{\top} y$$

### 2.2.2 Polynomial Regression

Extends linear regression by augmenting the feature vector with powers and cross-products: $\mathbf{x} \rightarrow [1, x, x^2, \dots, x^p]$. Provides non-linear fits — used to model dose-response curves in radiotherapy planning.

### 2.2.3 Ridge (L2) and Lasso (L1) Regression

Both regularize the weight vector to combat overfitting on small clinical cohorts.

### 2.2.4 Logistic-vs-Linear Decision Boundary

A logistic regression boundary is the level set where $\sigma(\mathbf{w}^{\top}\mathbf{x}+b)=0.5$, i.e., $\mathbf{w}^{\top}\mathbf{x}+b=0$ — a *linear* decision boundary. Hence the model is *linear in parameters*, even though the link function is non-linear.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **THE KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA TABLE — memorize all entries.**

| # | Concept | Formula | Notes / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Sigmoid (logistic) | $\sigma(z) = \dfrac{1}{1+e^{-z}}$ | Output $\in (0,1)$ |
| 2 | Softmax (multiclass) | $p(y=k\mid \mathbf{x}) = \dfrac{e^{z_k}}{\sum_{j} e^{z_j}}$ | $\sum_k p_k = 1$ |
| 3 | Cross-Entropy Loss | $\mathcal{L}_{CE} = -\dfrac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}\sum_{c=1}^{K} y_{ic}\log \hat{p}_{ic}$ | Unit: nats or bits |
| 4 | Hinge Loss (SVM) | $\mathcal{L}_{\text{hinge}} = \max(0, 1 - y \cdot f(\mathbf{x}))$ | $y \in \{-1,+1\}$ |
| 5 | MSE | $\text{MSE} = \dfrac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}(y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2$ | Unit: (target)² |
| 6 | MAE | $\text{MAE} = \dfrac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}\lvert y_i - \hat{y}_i \rvert$ | Same as target |
| 7 | RMSE | $\text{RMSE} = \sqrt{\text{MSE}}$ | Same as target |
| 8 | Coefficient of Determination | $R^2 = 1 - \dfrac{\sum_i (y_i-\hat{y}_i)^2}{\sum_i (y_i-\bar{y})^2}$ | $R^2 \leq 1$ |
| 9 | Ridge Objective | $\min_{\mathbf{w}} \lVert X\mathbf{w}-y \rVert_2^2 + \lambda \lVert \mathbf{w} \rVert_2^2$ | $\lambda > 0$ |
| 10 | Lasso Objective | $\min_{\mathbf{w}} \lVert X\mathbf{w}-y \rVert_2^2 + \lambda \lVert \mathbf{w} \rVert_1$ | induces sparsity |
| 11 | SVM Margin | $\gamma = \dfrac{2}{\lVert \mathbf{w} \rVert}$ | Maximize $\equiv$ minimize $\lVert \mathbf{w} \rVert$ |
| 12 | RBF Kernel | $\kappa(\mathbf{x}_i,\mathbf{x}_j) = \exp\!\left(-\gamma \lVert \mathbf{x}_i - \mathbf{x}_j \rVert^2\right)$ | $\gamma > 0$ |
| 13 | Entropy | $H(S) = -\sum_c p_c \log_2 p_c$ | bits |
| 14 | Information Gain | $\text{IG}(S,A) = H(S) - \sum_v \dfrac{\lvert S_v \rvert}{\lvert S \rvert} H(S_v)$ | $\geq 0$ |
| 15 | Accuracy | $\text{Acc} = \dfrac{TP+TN}{TP+TN+FP+FN}$ | $\in [0,1]$ |
| 16 | Precision | $\text{Prec} = \dfrac{TP}{TP+FP}$ | Clinical: PPV |
| 17 | Recall (Sensitivity) | $\text{Rec} = \dfrac{TP}{TP+FN}$ | Clinical: TPR |
| 18 | Specificity | $\text{Spec} = \dfrac{TN}{TN+FP}$ | Clinical: TNR |
| 19 | F1 Score | $F_1 = \dfrac{2 \cdot \text{Prec} \cdot \text{Rec}}{\text{Prec} + \text{Rec}}$ | Harmonic mean |
| 20 | AUC-ROC | Area under TPR vs FPR curve | $\in [0.5, 1]$ |
| 21 | Dice Coefficient | $\text{Dice} = \dfrac{2\lvert A \cap B \rvert}{\lvert A \rvert + \lvert B \rvert}$ | $\in [0,1]$ |
| 22 | k-Means Objective | $J = \sum_{i=1}^{k}\sum_{\mathbf{x}\in C_i}\lVert \mathbf{x}-\boldsymbol{\mu}_i \rVert^2$ | minimize $J$ |
| 23 | Gradient Descent Update | $\mathbf{w} \leftarrow \mathbf{w} - \eta \nabla_{\mathbf{w}}\mathcal{L}$ | $\eta$ = learning rate |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world engineering utility:** These formulas underpin FDA-cleared AI products such as **IDx-DR** (diabetic retinopathy, uses CNN classifier), **Viz.ai LVO** (stroke triage, uses CNN), and **Aidence Veye Lung Nodules** (regression + classification for nodule volumetry).

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Logistic Regression Cross-Entropy Loss

**Step 1.** Model the class-1 probability using the sigmoid:
$$p(y=1 \mid \mathbf{x}; \mathbf{w}, b) = \sigma(\mathbf{w}^{\top}\mathbf{x} + b) = \dfrac{1}{1 + e^{-(\mathbf{w}^{\top}\mathbf{x}+b)}}$$

**Step 2.** Write the likelihood over $N$ i.i.d. samples with binary labels $y_i \in \{0,1\}$:
$$L(\mathbf{w}, b) = \prod_{i=1}^{N} p(y_i \mid \mathbf{x}_i) = \prod_{i=1}^{N} \hat{p}_i^{\,y_i} (1-\hat{p}_i)^{1-y_i}$$

**Step 3.** Take the log-likelihood (monotonic transformation):
$$\ell(\mathbf{w}, b) = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \Big[ y_i \log \hat{p}_i + (1-y_i) \log(1-\hat{p}_i) \Big]$$

**Step 4.** Negate to obtain the loss to minimize:
$$\mathcal{L}_{CE} = -\dfrac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \Big[ y_i \log \hat{p}_i + (1-y_i) \log(1-\hat{p}_i) \Big]$$

**Step 5.** Substitute $\hat{p}_i = \sigma(z_i)$ and $1-\hat{p}_i = \sigma(-z_i)$, then use $\sigma'(z) = \sigma(z)(1-\sigma(z))$ to obtain the gradient:
$$\dfrac{\partial \mathcal{L}_{CE}}{\partial \mathbf{w}} = \dfrac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} (\hat{p}_i - y_i)\,\mathbf{x}_i$$

**Step 6.** Update via gradient descent:
$$\mathbf{w} \leftarrow \mathbf{w} - \eta \cdot \dfrac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}(\hat{p}_i - y_i)\,\mathbf{x}_i$$

## 3.2 Derivation: SVM Maximum-Margin Hyperplane

**Step 1.** Define the separating hyperplane: $\mathbf{w}^{\top}\mathbf{x} + b = 0$.

**Step 2.** Distance from any point $\mathbf{x}_i$ to the hyperplane:
$$d_i = \dfrac{\lvert \mathbf{w}^{\top}\mathbf{x}_i + b \rvert}{\lVert \mathbf{w} \rVert}$$

**Step 3.** Functional margin for correct classification: $y_i(\mathbf{w}^{\top}\mathbf{x}_i + b) \geq 1$ (canonical form).

**Step 4.** Geometric margin (smallest distance of any sample to the hyperplane):
$$\gamma = \min_i \dfrac{y_i(\mathbf{w}^{\top}\mathbf{x}_i + b)}{\lVert \mathbf{w} \rVert} = \dfrac{1}{\lVert \mathbf{w} \rVert}$$

**Step 5.** Maximize $\gamma$ subject to constraints:
$$\begin{aligned}
\max_{\mathbf{w},b} \quad & \dfrac{2}{\lVert \mathbf{w} \rVert} \\
\text{s.t.} \quad & y_i(\mathbf{w}^{\top}\mathbf{x}_i + b) \geq 1, \;\; i=1,\dots,N
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 6.** Equivalent convex minimization:
$$\begin{aligned}
\min_{\mathbf{w},b} \quad & \dfrac{1}{2}\lVert \mathbf{w} \rVert^2 \\
\text{s.t.} \quad & y_i(\mathbf{w}^{\top}\mathbf{x}_i + b) \geq 1, \;\; \forall i
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 7.** Introduce Lagrange multipliers $\alpha_i \geq 0$:
$$\mathcal{L}(\mathbf{w}, b, \boldsymbol{\alpha}) = \dfrac{1}{2}\lVert \mathbf{w} \rVert^2 - \sum_{i=1}^{N}\alpha_i \big[ y_i(\mathbf{w}^{\top}\mathbf{x}_i + b) - 1 \big]$$

**Step 8.** KKT conditions yield $\mathbf{w}^{*} = \sum_i \alpha_i^{*} y_i \mathbf{x}_i$, where only support vectors have $\alpha_i > 0$.

## 3.3 Derivation: Linear Regression via Normal Equation

**Step 1.** Hypothesis (with $b$ absorbed into $\mathbf{w}$): $\hat{\mathbf{y}} = X\mathbf{w}$, where $X \in \mathbb{R}^{N \times (d+1)}$.

**Step 2.** Sum-of-squared-errors objective:
$$J(\mathbf{w}) = \lVert X\mathbf{w} - \mathbf{y} \rVert_2^2 = (X\mathbf{w}-\mathbf{y})^{\top}(X\mathbf{w}-\mathbf{y})$$

**Step 3.** Expand:
$$J(\mathbf{w}) = \mathbf{w}^{\top}X^{\top}X\mathbf{w} - 2\mathbf{w}^{\top}X^{\top}\mathbf{y} + \mathbf{y}^{\top}\mathbf{y}$$

**Step 4.** Differentiate w.r.t. $\mathbf{w}$ and set to zero:
$$\nabla_{\mathbf{w}} J(\mathbf{w}) = 2X^{\top}X\mathbf{w} - 2X^{\top}\mathbf{y} = 0$$

**Step 5.** Solve the linear system:
$$\boxed{\;\mathbf{w}^{*} = (X^{\top}X)^{-1} X^{\top} \mathbf{y}\;}$$

The term $(X^{\top}X)^{-1}X^{\top}$ is the **Moore–Penrose pseudo-inverse** when $X$ is non-square.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Sample Confusion Matrix Walk-through

**Setup:** A mammography AI classifier tested on 200 suspicious lesions (80 malignant, 120 benign). Predictions: TP = 72, FN = 8, FP = 12, TN = 108.

**Compute Accuracy:**
$$\text{Acc} = \dfrac{TP+TN}{TP+TN+FP+FN} = \dfrac{72+108}{72+108+12+8} = \dfrac{180}{200} = 0.90$$

**Compute Precision (PPV):**
$$\text{Prec} = \dfrac{TP}{TP+FP} = \dfrac{72}{72+12} = \dfrac{72}{84} \approx 0.857$$

**Compute Recall (Sensitivity):**
$$\text{Rec} = \dfrac{TP}{TP+FN} = \dfrac{72}{72+8} = \dfrac{72}{80} = 0.90$$

**Compute Specificity:**
$$\text{Spec} = \dfrac{TN}{TN+FP} = \dfrac{108}{108+12} = 0.90$$

**Compute F1 Score:**
$$F_1 = \dfrac{2 \cdot \text{Prec} \cdot \text{Rec}}{\text{Prec} + \text{Rec}} = \dfrac{2 \times 0.857 \times 0.90}{0.857 + 0.90} \approx 0.878$$

## 3.5 Worked Regression Example — Predicting Tumor Volume

Suppose we have $N=5$ radiomics-derived training samples $(\mathbf{x}_i, y_i)$ where $\mathbf{x}_i \in \mathbb{R}^{2}$ and $y_i$ is the tumor volume in cm³. Ridge regression with $\lambda = 0.1$ minimizes:
$$J_{\text{ridge}}(\mathbf{w}) = \lVert X\mathbf{w}-\mathbf{y} \rVert_2^2 + 0.1 \lVert \mathbf{w} \rVert_2^2$$

Closed-form solution:
$$\mathbf{w}^{*} = (X^{\top}X + 0.1\, I)^{-1} X^{\top}\mathbf{y}$$

The diagonal inflation by $0.1\,I$ guarantees invertibility even when $X^{\top}X$ is rank-deficient (a frequent pathology in radiomics where $d \gg n$). This example illustrates exactly why regularization is mandatory in clinical regression.

## 3.6 Comparative Algorithmic Trade-off Matrix

| Algorithm | Strength | Weakness | Best Use Case in Medical Imaging |
|---|---|---|---|
| Logistic Regression | Interpretable, fast | Linear boundary only | Risk stratification, baselines |
| SVM (RBF) | Strong in HDLSS | Slow on $N > 10^4$ | Radiomics classification |
| k-NN | Zero training time | Costly inference, curse of dim | Quick prototyping |
| Decision Tree | Explainable rules | High variance | Clinical rule extraction |
| Random Forest | Robust, feature importance | Less interpretable | Multi-class diagnosis |
| CNN | State-of-art accuracy | Needs large data, black box | Direct image diagnosis |
| Linear Regression | Closed-form solution | Sensitive to outliers | Volume/biomarker estimation |
| Ridge/Lasso | Prevents overfitting | Hyperparameter tuning | Small clinical cohorts |

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]`
**Differentiate between classification and regression in the context of medical image analysis. Give one clinical example of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Classification** predicts a discrete categorical label; **Regression** predicts a continuous numerical value. **(1 mark)**
- Classification answers *"which class?"*, e.g., classifying a chest X-ray as **NORMAL** or **PNEUMONIA**. **(1 mark)**
- Regression answers *"how much?"*, e.g., estimating **tumor volume in mm³** from an MRI scan. **(1 mark)**

### **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - June 2024 Model]`
**Define the terms Sensitivity, Specificity, and Precision in medical image classification. Why is Sensitivity particularly critical in cancer screening?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Sensitivity** (Recall / TPR) $= \dfrac{TP}{TP+FN}$ — proportion of actual positives correctly identified. **(1 mark)**
- **Specificity** (TNR) $= \dfrac{TN}{TN+FP}$ — proportion of actual negatives correctly identified. **(1 mark)**
- **Precision** (PPV) $= \dfrac{TP}{TP+FP}$ — proportion of positive predictions that are truly positive. **(1 mark)**
- Sensitivity is critical in cancer screening because a **False Negative** means a missed cancer diagnosis — a life-threatening error. Maximizing sensitivity (recall) minimizes such misses, often at the cost of extra biopsies from false positives.

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]`

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **Support Vector Machine (SVM)** algorithm for binary classification. Derive the **margin maximization formulation** and state the role of the **kernel trick**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A mammography AI system produced the following confusion matrix on a 200-patient validation set: TP = 90, FN = 10, FP = 30, TN = 70. Compute **Accuracy, Precision, Recall, Specificity, and F1-score**. Comment on the model's clinical suitability. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — (a) SVM

**Definition (1 mark):** SVM is a maximum-margin classifier that finds the hyperplane separating two classes with the largest geometric distance to the nearest training points.

**Geometric Setup (2 marks):** Hyperplane: $\mathbf{w}^{\top}\mathbf{x}+b=0$. Margin $= \dfrac{2}{\lVert \mathbf{w} \rVert}$. Support vectors are the points lying on the margin boundaries $y_i(\mathbf{w}^{\top}\mathbf{x}_i+b)=\pm 1$.

**Formulation (2 marks):**
$$\min_{\mathbf{w},b} \;\dfrac{1}{2}\lVert \mathbf{w} \rVert^2 \quad \text{s.t.}\; y_i(\mathbf{w}^{\top}\mathbf{x}_i+b)\geq 1$$

**Kernel Trick (2 marks):** For non-linearly-separable data, replace $\mathbf{x}_i^{\top}\mathbf{x}_j$ with $\kappa(\mathbf{x}_i,\mathbf{x}_j)=\phi(\mathbf{x}_i)^{\top}\phi(\mathbf{x}_j)$. Common kernels: linear, polynomial, **RBF** $\kappa(\mathbf{x}_i,\mathbf{x}_j)=\exp(-\gamma\lVert \mathbf{x}_i-\mathbf{x}_j \rVert^2)$. The kernel trick avoids explicit computation in high-dimensional feature space.

#### Model Solution — (b) Confusion Matrix Computation

Given: $TP=90,\; FN=10,\; FP=30,\; TN=70$, Total $=200$.

$$\text{Accuracy} = \dfrac{90+70}{200} = \dfrac{160}{200} = 0.80 \quad \textbf{(1 mark)}$$

$$\text{Precision} = \dfrac{90}{90+30} = \dfrac{90}{120} = 0.75 \quad \textbf{(1 mark)}$$

$$\text{Recall (Sensitivity)} = \dfrac{90}{90+10} = \dfrac{90}{100} = 0.90 \quad \textbf{(1.5 marks)}$$

$$\text{Specificity} = \dfrac{70}{70+30} = \dfrac{70}{100} = 0.70 \quad \textbf{(1.5 marks)}$$

$$F_1 = \dfrac{2 \times 0.75 \times 0.90}{0.75 + 0.90} = \dfrac{1.35}{1.65} \approx 0.818 \quad \textbf{(1 mark)}$$

**Clinical Comment (1 mark):** Sensitivity = 0.90 means 10% of cancers are missed — still significant. Specificity = 0.70 implies 30 unnecessary biopsies per 100 healthy patients. The model is acceptable as a **second-reader** but **not** as a stand-alone diagnostic tool. Threshold tuning (lowering the decision threshold) is recommended to raise sensitivity above 0.95 for screening use.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - June 2024 Model]`

**(a)** Explain the **Logistic Regression** classifier. Derive the **cross-entropy loss** from the maximum-likelihood principle and write its gradient update rule. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare **Linear, Ridge (L2), and Lasso (L1)** regression with respect to their objective functions, closed-form solutions, and sparsity of the weight vector. Explain why regularization is essential in medical image regression tasks with $d > n$. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — (a) Logistic Regression

**Algorithm (2 marks):** Linear score $z=\mathbf{w}^{\top}\mathbf{x}+b$ is passed through sigmoid $\sigma(z)=\dfrac{1}{1+e^{-z}}$ to give probability $p(y=1\mid \mathbf{x})$. Decision: predict class 1 if $p\geq 0.5$.

**Likelihood (2 marks):**
$$L(\mathbf{w},b) = \prod_{i=1}^{N} \hat{p}_i^{y_i}(1-\hat{p}_i)^{1-y_i}$$

**Cross-Entropy Loss (2 marks):**
$$\mathcal{L}_{CE} = -\dfrac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}\big[y_i\log \hat{p}_i + (1-y_i)\log(1-\hat{p}_i)\big]$$

**Gradient (1 mark):**
$$\nabla_{\mathbf{w}}\mathcal{L}_{CE} = \dfrac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}(\hat{p}_i - y_i)\mathbf{x}_i, \quad \mathbf{w} \leftarrow \mathbf{w} - \eta \nabla_{\mathbf{w}}\mathcal{L}_{CE}$$

#### Model Solution — (b) Regression Comparison

**Linear Regression (2 marks):**
- Objective: $\min_{\mathbf{w}} \lVert X\mathbf{w}-\mathbf{y} \rVert_2^2$.
- Solution: $\mathbf{w}^{*}=(X^{\top}X)^{-1}X^{\top}\mathbf{y}$ (Normal Equation).
- Weights: dense, no sparsity.

**Ridge (L2) (2 marks):**
- Objective: $\min_{\mathbf{w}} \lVert X\mathbf{w}-\mathbf{y} \rVert_2^2 + \lambda \lVert \mathbf{w} \rVert_2^2$.
- Solution: $\mathbf{w}^{*}=(X^{\top}X + \lambda I)^{-1}X^{\top}\mathbf{y}$.
