# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Explain the role of Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) in medical image classification. Mention any two key components.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) are deep learning architectures designed specifically for processing image data and have revolutionized medical image classification.

**Key Roles:**
1. **Automatic Feature Extraction:** Unlike traditional ML methods requiring manual feature engineering, CNNs automatically learn hierarchical features — edges in early layers, textures in middle layers, and complex anatomical patterns in deeper layers.
2. **Spatial Hierarchy Preservation:** Through convolution and pooling operations, CNNs preserve the 2D/3D spatial relationships critical for medical images (e.g., tumor boundaries).
3. **Translation Invariance:** Once a CNN learns to detect a feature (e.g., a lung nodule), it can identify the same feature anywhere in the image.

**Two Key Components:**
- **Convolutional Layer:** Applies learnable filters/kernels to extract local features via the convolution operation $F = I * K$.
- **Pooling Layer:** Reduces spatial dimensionality (e.g., max-pooling selects the maximum value in a $2\times2$ window), providing translation invariance and reducing computational cost.

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define Dice Coefficient and explain its importance in medical image segmentation evaluation.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The **Dice Similarity Coefficient (DSC)** is a statistical metric used to gauge the similarity between two samples, defined as:

$$
\text{Dice} = \dfrac{2 \vert P \cap G \vert}{\vert P \vert + \vert G \vert}
$$

where $P$ is the predicted segmentation mask and $G$ is the ground truth annotation.

**Importance in Medical Image Segmentation:**
1. **Region Overlap Quantification:** It measures the spatial overlap between predicted and actual tumor/organ regions on a scale of 0 (no overlap) to 1 (perfect overlap).
2. **Robust to Class Imbalance:** Unlike accuracy, Dice works well even when the foreground (e.g., tumor) constitutes only 1-2% of the image — a common scenario in medical images.
3. **Clinical Relevance:** A Dice score > 0.7 is generally considered acceptable for tumor segmentation; > 0.85 indicates high-quality automated delineation suitable for radiotherapy planning.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As per KTU 2024 ESE regulations, the student must answer **ONE full question** from Module 4. Each long-answer question carries **14 marks**, typically split into two sub-parts of 7 marks each.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the end-to-end pipeline of an AI-based medical image processing system. List the major stages and describe any three preprocessing techniques in detail. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the architecture of the U-Net model for medical image segmentation. Explain the role of skip connections and derive the Dice loss function used to train it. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Question A**

**(a) AI Medical Image Processing Pipeline (7 Marks)**

The AI-based medical image processing pipeline consists of the following sequential stages:

| **Stage** | **Purpose** | **Key Techniques** |
|---|---|---|
| **1. Image Acquisition** | Capture raw medical data from modalities (CT, MRI, X-Ray, Ultrasound, PET) | DICOM/NIfTI file formats, voxel intensity sampling |
| **2. Preprocessing** | Standardize image quality for consistent model input | Normalization, denoising, bias correction, resampling |
| **3. Augmentation** | Expand dataset size to prevent overfitting | Rotation, flipping, elastic deformation, intensity jittering |
| **4. Dataset Splitting** | Organize data for unbiased evaluation | Train (70%), Validation (15%), Test (15%) |
| **5. Model Training** | Learn discriminative features via backpropagation | CNN, U-Net, ResNet with cross-entropy/Dice loss |
| **6. Inference & Evaluation** | Generate predictions and quantify performance | Confusion matrix, Dice, IoU, Sensitivity/Specificity |
| **7. Clinical Deployment** | Integrate into hospital PACS/RIS systems | FDA/CE certification, DICOM compliance |

**Three Preprocessing Techniques in Detail:**

**(i) Intensity Normalization:**
Medical images from different scanners have varying intensity ranges. For CT scans, **Hounsfield Unit (HU) windowing** maps raw intensities to a consistent range:

$$
HU = 1000 \cdot \dfrac{\mu_{tissue} - \mu_{water}}{\mu_{water} - \mu_{air}}
$$

This is clipped to a clinically relevant window (e.g., brain: 0–80 HU) and rescaled to [0, 1] for neural network input.

**(ii) Noise Reduction:**
Gaussian noise, speckle noise (ultrasound), and Rician noise (MRI) degrade diagnostic quality. **Non-Local Means (NLM) denoising** preserves edges while smoothing homogeneous regions by averaging similar patches across the image.

**(iii) Bias Field Correction (MRI):**
MRI intensities suffer from low-frequency intensity non-uniformity due to magnetic field inhomogeneities. The **N4ITK algorithm** estimates and removes this bias field, ensuring consistent tissue intensities.

---

**(b) U-Net Architecture & Dice Loss Derivation (7 Marks)**

**U-Net Architecture Overview:**
U-Net (Ronneberger et al., 2015) is a symmetric encoder-decoder network specifically designed for biomedical image segmentation. Its name derives from its characteristic **U-shaped** structure.

**Encoder (Contracting Path):**
- Repeated application of two $3 \times 3$ convolutions, each followed by ReLU activation and batch normalization
- $2 \times 2$ max-pooling with stride 2 for downsampling
- Feature channels double at each level: 64 → 128 → 256 → 512 → 1024

**Decoder (Expanding Path):**
- $2 \times 2$ transposed convolution (up-convolution) for upsampling
- Concatenation with corresponding encoder feature map (skip connection)
- Two $3 \times 3$ convolutions with ReLU activation

**Final Layer:** $1 \times 1$ convolution maps the 64-channel feature map to the desired number of segmentation classes.

**Role of Skip Connections:**
Skip connections directly concatenate feature maps from the encoder to the corresponding decoder level. They serve two critical purposes:
1. **Recover Spatial Information:** Pooling operations discard fine-grained localization. Skip connections restore pixel-level precision needed for accurate boundary delineation.
2. **Enable Multi-Scale Feature Fusion:** Combines low-level features (edges, textures) with high-level semantic features (organ identity), improving segmentation accuracy.

**Dice Loss Derivation:**

The Dice Coefficient for binary segmentation is:

$$
\text{Dice} = \dfrac{2 \vert P \cap G \vert}{\vert P \vert + \vert G \vert}
$$

Expressed in differentiable form for neural network training:

$$
\text{Dice}(P, G) = \dfrac{2 \sum_{i=1}^{N} p_i g_i + \epsilon}{\sum_{i=1}^{N} p_i + \sum_{i=1}^{N} g_i + \epsilon}
$$

where $\epsilon$ is a small constant (e.g., $10^{-7}$) added to prevent division by zero and ensure numerical stability in regions with no foreground pixels.

The **Dice Loss** is defined as:

$$
\mathcal{L}_{Dice} = 1 - \text{Dice}(P, G) = 1 - \dfrac{2 \sum_{i=1}^{N} p_i g_i + \epsilon}{\sum_{i=1}^{N} p_i + \sum_{i=1}^{N} g_i + \epsilon}
$$

**Properties:**
- $\mathcal{L}_{Dice} = 0$ when prediction perfectly matches ground truth
- $\mathcal{L}_{Dice} \to 1$ when there is no overlap
- It is bounded between 0 and 1, making training stable

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the Confusion Matrix in medical image classification. Define Sensitivity, Specificity, Precision, and F1-Score with formulas. Given TP=92, FN=8, FP=5, TN=95, compute all four metrics and interpret the clinical significance. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the application of Transfer Learning in medical imaging. Explain how pre-trained networks like ResNet and Inception are adapted for limited medical datasets. List three clinical case studies. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Question B**

**(a) Confusion Matrix & Clinical Metrics (7 Marks)**

A **Confusion Matrix** is a $2 \times 2$ table that summarizes the performance of a binary classifier by comparing predicted labels against ground truth labels:

|  | **Predicted Positive** | **Predicted Negative** |
|---|---|---|
| **Actual Positive** | True Positive (TP) | False Negative (FN) |
| **Actual Negative** | False Positive (FP) | True Negative (TN) |

**Metric Definitions with Formulas:**

- **Sensitivity (Recall / True Positive Rate):**

$$
\text{Sensitivity} = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FN}
$$

Measures the proportion of actual positives correctly identified. Critical for screening applications (e.g., cancer detection — missing a positive case is dangerous).

- **Specificity (True Negative Rate):**

$$
\text{Specificity} = \dfrac{TN}{TN + FP}
$$

Measures the proportion of actual negatives correctly identified. Important for avoiding false alarms and unnecessary biopsies.

- **Precision (Positive Predictive Value):**

$$
\text{Precision} = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FP}
$$

Measures the proportion of positive predictions that are actually correct.

- **F1-Score:**

$$
F_1 = 2 \cdot \dfrac{\text{Precision} \cdot \text{Recall}}{\text{Precision} + \text{Recall}}
$$

Harmonic mean of precision and recall — balances both concerns in a single metric.

**Numerical Computation (TP=92, FN=8, FP=5, TN=95):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Sensitivity} &= \dfrac{92}{92 + 8} = \dfrac{92}{100} = 0.92 \; (92\%) \\
\text{Specificity} &= \dfrac{95}{95 + 5} = \dfrac{95}{100} = 0.95 \; (95\%) \\
\text{Precision} &= \dfrac{92}{92 + 5} = \dfrac{92}{97} \approx 0.948 \; (94.8\%) \\
F_1 &= 2 \cdot \dfrac{0.948 \times 0.92}{0.948 + 0.92} = 2 \cdot \dfrac{0.872}{1.868} \approx 0.934 \; (93.4\%)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Clinical Significance:**
- **92% Sensitivity** means the model correctly flags 92 out of 100 actual tumor cases — clinically acceptable for early screening.
- **95% Specificity** means only 5% of healthy patients are falsely alarmed — reducing unnecessary follow-up scans.
- **94.8% Precision** indicates high reliability of positive predictions — minimizes patient anxiety from false positives.
- **93.4% F1-Score** confirms balanced performance across precision and recall — suitable for clinical deployment.

---

**(b) Transfer Learning in Medical Imaging (7 Marks)**

**Concept:** Transfer Learning leverages knowledge learned from large, general datasets (e.g., ImageNet with 14 million images) and applies it to specialized domains with limited data — a perfect match for medical imaging where annotated datasets are scarce.

**Working Methodology:**

**Step 1 — Select Pre-trained Backbone:**
Popular architectures include:
- **ResNet-50/101:** Residual connections enable training of very deep networks (>100 layers) by mitigating vanishing gradients
- **Inception-v3:** Multi-scale convolutional kernels ($1\times1$, $3\times3$, $5\times5$) capture features at different resolutions simultaneously
- **VGG-16/19:** Simple, uniform architecture with stacked $3\times3$ convolutions
- **EfficientNet:** Compound scaling balances network depth, width, and resolution

**Step 2 — Freeze Early Layers:**
The initial convolutional layers learn generic features (edges, color gradients, textures) applicable across domains. These are **frozen** to preserve pre-trained knowledge and reduce the number of trainable parameters.

$$
\theta_{\text{frozen}} = \text{constant} \quad \text{during backpropagation}
$$

**Step 3 — Replace Final Classification Layer:**
The original 1000-class ImageNet softmax layer is replaced with a new layer matching the medical task:
- Binary classification (tumor/normal): 2 outputs with sigmoid
- Multi-class (e.g., 5 skin lesion types): 5 outputs with softmax

**Step 4 — Fine-tune on Medical Dataset:**
The new top layers (and optionally last few backbone blocks) are trained on the medical dataset at a **lower learning rate** (e.g., $10^{-4}$ vs $10^{-2}$) to avoid catastrophic forgetting of pre-trained weights.

**Three Clinical Case Studies:**

1. **Diabetic Retinopathy Detection (Gulshan et al., JAMA 2016):** Used Inception-v3 pre-trained on ImageNet, fine-tuned on ~88,000 retinal fundus images. Achieved sensitivity 87–90% and specificity 98% — comparable to ophthalmologists.

2. **Pneumonia Detection from Chest X-Rays (Rajpurkar et al., 2017 — CheXNet):** Used DenseNet-121 pre-trained on ImageNet to detect pneumonia from chest X-rays, achieving F1-score of 0.435 (better than radiologist average of 0.387).

3. **Skin Lesion Classification (Esteva et al., Nature 2017):** Used Inception-v3 fine-tuned on ~130,000 dermatoscopic images to classify skin cancer. Matched the performance of 21 board-certified dermatologists across binary and multi-class tasks.

**Advantages for Medical AI:**
- Requires only hundreds to a few thousand annotated medical images (vs. millions from scratch)
- Reduces training time from weeks to hours
- Improves generalization on small datasets
- Enables rapid prototyping for rare diseases

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
> 
> 1. **Formula Confusion (Dice vs IoU):** Many students interchange Dice and IoU formulas. **Dice** has a factor of **2 in the numerator**; IoU does not. Memorize both formulas separately.
> 
> 2. **Confusion Matrix Sign Errors:** Students often swap FP/FN or misplace them in the matrix. Remember: **F**alse **P**ositive = "False Alarm" (predicts positive but is actually negative); **F**alse **N**egative = "Missed Case" (predicts negative but is actually positive).
> 
> 3. **Missing Clinical Context:** KTU examiners award marks for relating AI techniques to **real clinical scenarios**. Don't just define CNN — explain *why* it suits medical images (spatial locality, translation invariance).
> 
> 4. **No Diagrams:** A neat labeled block diagram is worth **2-3 marks** in 14-mark questions. Always include U-Net architecture, pipeline flowchart, or confusion matrix.
> 
> 5. **Skipping Skip Connections:** U-Net's skip connections are the most frequently asked architecture detail. Explain their **dual role** — spatial recovery AND multi-scale feature fusion.
> 
> 6. **Hounsfield Unit Errors:** Many students forget that **air = -1000 HU** and **water = 0 HU**. This is a high-frequency short-answer trap.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 4: AI in Medical Image Processing**

### **Core Concepts**
- ✅ Medical Image Processing = acquisition + preprocessing + analysis of clinical images
- ✅ AI = automated diagnosis via ML/DL replacing manual feature engineering
- ✅ Pipeline stages: Acquisition → Preprocessing → Augmentation → Training → Inference → Evaluation

### **Architectures**
- ✅ **CNN** = Convolution + Pooling + Fully Connected layers; learns hierarchical features
- ✅ **U-Net** = Symmetric encoder-decoder with **skip connections**; standard for medical segmentation
- ✅ **ResNet** = Residual/skip connections inside CNN; enables very deep networks
- ✅ **Transfer Learning** = Adapt ImageNet pre-trained models to medical tasks

### **Formulas (Must Memorize)**
- ✅ Dice Coefficient: $\dfrac{2 \vert P \cap G \vert}{\vert P \vert + \vert G \vert}$
- ✅ IoU: $\dfrac{\vert P \cap G \vert}{\vert P \cup G \vert}$
- ✅ Sensitivity: $\dfrac{TP}{TP+FN}$
- ✅ Specificity: $\dfrac{TN}{TN+FP}$
- ✅ Cross-Entropy Loss: $-\sum y \log(\hat{y})$
- ✅ Hounsfield Units: $1000 \cdot \dfrac{\mu_{tissue} - \mu_{water}}{\mu_{water} - \mu_{air}}$

### **Clinical Applications**
- ✅ Tumor detection (CT/MRI), Pneumonia (X-ray), Diabetic Retinopathy (Fundus), Skin Cancer (Dermatoscopy), Brain Stroke (CT), Cardiac Analysis (Echo)

### **Evaluation Benchmarks**
- ✅ Dice > 0.70 = acceptable segmentation
- ✅ Sensitivity > 90% = clinical-grade screening
- ✅ AUC-ROC > 0.90 = strong discriminative performance

### **Challenges**
- ✅ Limited annotated data → use augmentation, transfer learning, GANs
- ✅ Class imbalance → use Dice Loss, weighted cross-entropy
- ✅ Interpretability → use Grad-CAM, saliency maps
- ✅ Regulatory approval (FDA/CE) for clinical deployment
- ✅ Generalization across multi-center, multi-scanner datasets

### **Key Takeaway**
> **AI in medical imaging is not replacing radiologists — it is augmenting them with quantitative, reproducible, and ultra-fast second-opinion support, ultimately improving diagnostic accuracy and patient outcomes.**
